This is bullseye information for us to utilize today. Whumpfing collapses, shooting cracks, and recent avalanches are all signs of unstable snow. Today, expect to observe these warning signals more commonly on shaded aspects where slabs rest over weak layers. Slabs can be triggered from a distance and propagate surprisingly far distances. Keep an eye out for wind-drifted snow in atypical locations. Look for dune-shaped drifts and wind-textured surfaces. Although remote weather stations reported light wind speeds, easterly winds are notorious for drifting snow where they gain momentum as they channel through terrain features.'}]
bottom_line: Small to large avalanches failed on slopes above 6500 feet in the past 48 hours. Shooting cracks, recent avalanches, and audible collapses should point you to lower-angle terrain. Avalanches may be triggered from afar and wrap around terrain features. Give the snowpack time to adjust before riding in steep, consequential terrain.
discussion:
We have kept the danger rating elevated in the Swan and Flathead Ranges.  Although we suspect natural avalanche activity to be over, human-triggered avalanches may still be likely. Conditions will be more dangerous near the crests of the Flathead Range and Swan Range where more snow fell. This recent snow landed on weak layers that may be slow to gain strength. 
Recent storm totals vary throughout the forecast region. Below are Snow Water Equivalent (SWE) totals starting February 8th:
Noisy Basin in the Swan Range: 4.8 inches
Stahl in the northern Whitefish Range: 2.5 inches
Whitefish Mountain Resort in the southern Whitefish Range: 1.6 inches
Flattop in Central Glacier National Park: 2 inches *The Flathead Range, especially along the crest in the Middle Fork Corridor, likely received more precipitation.
Southwesterly winds during the storm were generally unimpressive. Blase did find wind-thickened slabs along the crest of the Swan Range on Monday. I’d suspect to find similar conditions along other exposed ridgelines. Winds flipped directions to the east yesterday. Easterly winds can wreak havoc on lower and middle-elevation slopes. Winds commonly channel through valleys, leaving some areas scoured and drifted, with other valleys untouched. Easterly winds are going to ramp up to transportable speeds this evening. If you are out late or planning an evening ride, pay close attention to wind-drifting snow. Slabs will become more sensitive to the weight of a rider.
Recent snow landed on a variety of weak surfaces that vary by aspect. Near-surface facets and surface hoar exist on shaded slopes. We suspect these will be the most sensitive and dangerous slopes at the moment. These are the same slopes that remotely triggered avalanches have failed on.
A crust/facet/crust combination exists on various solar aspects. This structure has been reported in all three ranges. It is more difficult to predict when these aspects will become active. The upper crust allows the weak layer to withstand a larger load before collapsing. This structure can produce very large avalanches IF it receives a substantial loading event.
Keep an eye on the sun and the surface snow IF temperatures become warmer than forecasted. Rollerballs, pinwheels, and point releases out of steep, rocky terrain indicate an impending Wet Loose cycle. Cold air should limit this problem today.

More info at flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

Remember, all of the new snow in the last 48 hours has fallen on a faceted crust from early February.\xa0 Any slab that fails on this layer will likely extend much further than expected.\xa0 '}]
bottom_line: New snow and recently drifted snow have developed dangerous avalanche conditions.  Soft slabs 1-2 feet thick will be reactive to the weight of a skier or snowmachine.  Lower your risk by giving steep slopes plenty of room.  Avalanches that fail today could be larger than you anticipate.
discussion:
Skunked, kind of.  While we all were hoping to wake up this morning and find weather stations reporting another foot of snow overnight, the faucet was turned off.   24-hour snowfall totals in the Whitefish range reported 3-9 inches, the Flathead range saw 4-7 inches and Noisey Basin received 6 inches.  These numbers account for recent snowpack settlement, so storm totals are likely much greater.  The storm put down an impressive total of 2.7 inches of water at Noisy Basin and 1.7 inches at Stahl Peak. 
The problem today encapsulates many things.  Winds have thickened slabs and drifted snow along ridgelines.  Over a foot of snow has fallen in the last 48 hours in sheltered terrain.  This new snow fell on facets above a very thick crust from early February.  There is complexity today as the snowpack adjusts to the recent loading.
Confidence is high that all aspects and elevations in the forecast area were affected by unseasonably warm weather in late January.  Several inches of light snow then fell making travel difficult and sluffing snow a problem.  This problematic soft snow faceted above the crust.  Now, today, it's buried by 1 to 2 feet of recent snow.  Avalanches that fail at this interface may extend around terrain features and be triggered from further away.

Signs of recent avalanches, shooting cracks, or whumpfs should direct you toward lower-angle terrain free of overhead hazards. NOTE: New snow is landing on weak surfaces. This may cause avalanches to propagate wider distances or be triggered remotely. Today is a day to have a "Stepping Back" mindset.'}]
bottom_line: Dangerous avalanche conditions are developing at upper elevations. New snow and wind will form slabs large enough to bury and injure a person. Stick to lower angle slopes if you see 8 or more inches fall throughout the day or see signs of shooting cracks and recent avalanches. Avoid areas of wind-drifting snow and be wary of traveling in the runouts of large avalanche paths.
discussion:
Conditions are changing! As conditions change, so should our travel habits. Plan to dial back to more conservative terrain choices throughout the day. New and recent snow is piling up and landing on a weak layer of near-surface facets above a slick crust. This may cause avalanches to break wider distances and in unexpected ways.
Today’s avalanche forecast relies on today’s precipitation forecast. There is high confidence that the forecast area will receive anywhere from .5 to 1 inch of Snow Water Equivalent (SWE) by Tuesday Morning. This can translate into upwards of 1 to 2 feet of snow. Although the liquid water amounts may not be overly impressive, snowfall rates of 1 inch or more an hour can rapidly increase the avalanche danger. Forecasted snowfall rates like this make it even more important to pay close attention to changing conditions. The avalanche danger WILL rise throughout the day with continued snowfall.
Precipitation amounts vary throughout the forecast area and within each forecast region. The Swan Range will see the higher end of precipitation totals. Models show precipitation favoring terrain west of Flathead Lake, so expect the avalanche danger to increase rapidly the further south you travel. The Flathead Range should catch any spillover from the Swan Range. I’d expect to see snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches in the Flathead Range. The crest of the range will likely wring out the remaining moisture, so expect less snow in southern and central Glacier National Park. The Northern Whitefish Range picked up close to an inch of water yesterday (Sunday) and may be favored with today's snow as well. Areas like Ten Lakes, Red Meadow Pass, and Gibraltar Ridge will see elevated avalanche danger.

These features will be larger, thicker, and more widespread at upper elevations while only found in the most wind-prone locations at middle elevations. Look for them downwind of ridgelines and saddles and on the sidewalls of gullies.\xa0 Look for rippled surfaces, hollow-sounding stiff snow, and cracks shooting out from your boards or machine. Blowing snow and cornices point toward the direction of loading. Terrain sheltered from the wind will offer a safer and better riding experience.'}]
bottom_line: Recent snow and increasing southwest winds will form fresh slabs above 5000 feet. Look for blowing snow, dune-shaped drifts, or textured surface snow as visual clues. You can reduce your risk of triggering a slide by avoiding steep, rocky, wind-affected terrain.
discussion:
As of 5 AM, weather stations report an uptick in southwesterly winds that will redistribute the recent unconsolidated snow. To account for these winds, we’ve transitioned our primary avalanche problem from Storm Slab to Wind Slab. Above 6500 feet, the snow available for transport will develop larger and more widespread slabs, while middle elevations will likely have less snow and wind, making for more isolated hazards. 
We dropped our Loose Dry problem today, however keep in mind that fast-running sluffs in steep terrain can still catch you off guard.  Before committing to a steep line, consider the terrain below you and the consequences of taking a ride into places you don’t want to go.
Observers from Saturday reported 3 to 12 inches of cohesionless snow over a stout and supportable crust formed in early February. Varying depths across the region favored upper elevations, and many observations described sluff management as their primary hazard. On steep slopes, observers were challenged by sluffing and sliding on the slick crust. For one party in the northern Whitefish Range, a rider was caught and carried 75 feet, but was thankfully not buried and escaped uninjured. Other observers in the southern Whitefish Range (Example 1, Example 2, Example 3) confirmed a loose, dry problem that was straightforward to manage.
In the Skyland area, riders reported isolated cracking, wind gusts transporting snow, and warming southern aspects from solar input. This contrasted conditions in the Swan Range, where skies were mostly cloudy, and skiers and riders reported powder turns above 5200 feet. As we enter the workweek, the avalanche danger is expected to rise due to new snow overnight, with accumulations estimated to be 6 to 8 inches or more in favored locations.
Upcoming event: Flathead Avalanche Friends Fest, February 21-24. Beacons & Brews, Name That Tune/Name That Avalanche, Workshop Night, Hot Dog Days, and Ultimate Après.  Check out 4 days of events: flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

bottom_line: You can still trigger a slab avalanche in both sheltered and wind-affected terrain. Loose snow sluffs are likely in steep terrain and may run naturally on sunny slopes. Limit your risk by reducing the time you spend traveling above terrain traps like gullies, cliffs, or exposed rocks and trees.
discussion:
As I review the weather and observations this morning, a theme emerges:  Winter is (kind of) back! Recent snowfall and seasonable temperatures have greatly improved the riding throughout the advisory area. However, in most cases, there is a prerequisite brush thrashing (obs 1, obs 2 , obs 3).
This was also the case during a FAC visit to Anna Creek on the East Side of the Swan Range. Throughout our travels, we noted generally unconsolidated snow resting on an impressively stout melt-freeze crust. We were able to kick off fast-running sluffs in extreme terrain on shaded slopes and found a cohesive slab in wind-stiffened snow at the entrance to a couloir. Both of these hazards were easily managed at the time, however, we acknowledged the potential for the faceting surface snow to become problematic once buried. Southeast aspects were particularly interesting as they harbored a crust/facet combination from warming Tuesday/Wednesday this week. Blase and Jeff noted this same structure East of WMR. This structure will be able to support a greater load and may result in larger avalanches once it reaches its limit.

bottom_line:
Fresh snowfall and wind have increased the avalanche danger. Riders may encounter slabs reactive to their weight in both wind-affected and sheltered areas. Expect to trigger loose snow slides on many steep slopes. Enjoy today's powder on lower-angle terrain so you can do the same tomorrow.
The hazard is highest in the Swan Range, where more snow fell.
discussion:
Snowfall Thursday and overnight into Friday morning favored the Swan Range, with over 1 inch of SWE and 7+ inches at Noisy Basin. As of 0500, most other mountain locations had picked up 3-6+ inches with 0.7 inches of SWE at Stryker Ridge in the Whitefish Range. Winds shifted back to the southwest yesterday and are generally light with moderate gusts. The wind is forecast to blow from the north or northeast as a low develops east of the Divide today. Temperatures, thankfully, have fallen back to a cool, seasonable range.
The new snow and wind have bumped the avalanche danger following two days of LOW danger.  The hazard is highest in the Swan Range, where more snow accumulates, and the winds are stronger. The Flathead Range and southern Glacier Park have also done well, with the Whitefish Range lagging. Snowfall should taper off through the day, but fresh slabs will continue forming in wind-affected terrain at upper elevations.
The storm was accompanied by intense periods of precipitation. The Noisy Basin SNOTEL recorded 0.5 inches of SWE (4+ inches) in a two-hour period overnight. Snowfall rates greater than 1 inch per hour generally create instability and possible natural avalanche activity. We have rolled a storm and wind slab problem together to mitigate uncertainty. Storm slabs are problem #1 in the Swan and Flathead zones, while loose dry is the #1 problem in the Whitefish Range.
Yesterday (Thursday), Rob, Cam, and Guy explored the terrain around the Anna Creek cabin on the east side of the Swan. They reported having to manage their sluff on steep, northerly aspects where 6-8 inches of faceted, loose surface snow moved downhill easily. Blase and Jeff encountered similar conditions on their tour east of the WMR ski area.

author: Mark Dundas
bottom_line: Recent benign weather has resulted in a generally stable snowpack. On isolated, steep, northerly slopes, you could still trigger a thin, loose slide that may knock you off your feet or encounter a small slab of wind-drifted snow reactive to your weight. Snow returns overnight into Friday with an increasing hazard.
discussion:
Due to the recent weather and snowpack conditions, we are not listing any avalanche problems today. This is unusual, especially for early February. A summary of the impact of the past few days on the snowpack is listed below.
Tuesday and Wednesday's dry, sunny weather initiated small, wet loose avalanches on solar aspects in the snowfall from earlier in the week and over the weekend (Observation 1, Observation 2). This melting formed a crust on many sunny aspects (Observation). Today's cloudy and cooler conditions will limit the crust's ability to break down and shut down any potential for wet loose slides.
On shaded slopes, thin, dry loose slides occurred naturally and were triggered by riders over the past few days (Observation 1, Observation 2). There remains a chance that you could trigger a small sluff in steep, northerly terrain. These should be easy to manage but could become a problem in technical terrain if a rider is knocked off their machine or boards. 
Shifting winds earlier in the week initially transported the new snow into thin slabs on various aspects. The winds have quieted down the past 48 hours, and we feel slabs have gained strength and are generally unreactive to triggering by a rider. Look for signs of wind-affected snow at upper elevations, such as dunes or drifts beneath ridgelines and in gullies. Cracking in this snow indicates instability, directing you to sheltered or lower-angle terrain. 
Snow will develop later today and into Friday. We expect several inches at all mountain locations, with the Swan Range favored. The avalanche danger will rise overnight, with new snow avalanche problems such as wind slabs and dry loose returning tomorrow.
Although the riding conditions are less than ideal in the Flathead, at least we are not dealing with a nasty persistent slab problem that southwest Montana and the Gallatin National Forest Avalanche Center have. The lower half of their snowpack is faceted (sugar) snow. It has been reactive to nearly every load placed onto it by small storms, resulting in a multi-day elevated avalanche hazard.
Upcoming event: Flathead Avalanche Friends Fest, February 21-24. Beacons & Brews, Name That Tune/Name That Avalanche, Workshop Night, Hot Dog Days, and Ultimate Après.  Check out 4 days of events: flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

Signs of cracking around your board or machine should steer you to areas sheltered from the wind. A thin surface crust will eliminate the chances of triggering a Dry Loose avalanche.\xa0'}]
bottom_line: Today’s avalanche problems are generally small and easy to manage. Expect to trigger Dry Loose avalanches in very steep, northerly-facing terrain. Slabs of drifted snow exist downwind of exposed ridgelines above 6500 feet. Steer around pillowy-looking surfaces and dune-shaped drifts.
discussion:
Most days an avalanche forecast is only as good as a weather forecast. Yesterday was one of those days. Cloud cover opened to clear skies and freezing levels climbed higher than expected, resulting in a round of natural loose wet avalanches on sunbaked slopes. So far, reported avalanche sizes were primarily small (D1), with a couple of large (D2) slides in longer running paths. Expect to find a sun crust on south and west aspects.
Reports of rider-triggered Dry Loose avalanches continue to trickle in, hence the reason for bringing it back to the problem list. This problem is primarily listed for those of you looking to ride in very steep terrain. Since solar aspects were cooked by the sun yesterday, I’d expect this problem to be confined to shaded slopes at middle and upper elevations. Any triggered avalanche should be predictable and easy to manage with slope cuts or other defensive riding strategies.
Wind speeds over the last 24 hours were calm and variable. Slabs of drifted snow will be two to three days old and difficult to trigger. Tuck in below or ride around wind-drifted slabs if your travels take you high into the alpine or into consequential terrain.
We removed Persistent Slab as a listed hazard on Monday. You can still find soft facets around a hard crust on many middle and upper-elevation slopes. Recent warm temperatures allowed these facets to gain strength. A supportable crust near the surface also adds strength to the snowpack by reducing the chances of collapsing the weak layer. If your travels take you to the highest elevations in the forecast area, it is still worth digging to look for this structure, especially if you don’t find a supportable crust beneath your feet or machine.

author: Cam Johnson
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wind Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'northwest'], 'middle': [], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'Expect to find soft slabs up to 1 foot thick in wind-loaded terrain features at upper elevations. These terrain features include slopes below exposed ridgelines and on the sidewalls of gullies. Varying wind directions over the last few days formed slabs on a variety of aspects. The painted distribution identifies recently formed slabs of drifted snow that will be more sensitive to the weight of a rider. This does not mean it is impossible to trigger an older, and more stubborn Wind Slab on southerly-facing slopes. Look for textured surfaces and dune-shaped drifts for evidence this problem exists. Signs of cracking around your board or machine should steer you to areas sheltered from the wind.'}]
bottom_line: Winds have drifted snow into fresh slabs on varying aspects above 6500 feet. Steer around dune-shaped drifts on your hunt for soft snow. Recent observations generally describe conditions as “a little more than dust on crust.”
discussion:
Roughly 3 to 8 inches of new and recent snow blankets a hard crust throughout the forecast area. Recent signs of unstable snow are limited to new snow problems. Observers have triggered Loose Dry avalanches in steep terrain, while others found small Wind Slabs on exposed slopes. Ski Patrol at Whitefish Mountain Resort triggered Wind Slabs 6 to 10 inches thick with ski cuts and explosives on Sunday. 
Wind directions have shifted from northeast to southwest over the last couple of days. This shift in winds formed two generations of recent slabs. The older, more stubborn slab of drifted snow will be in areas favorable to northeast winds. Observers reported finding slabs on south and west aspects yesterday (Monday). The more recent and more sensitive slab will be on slopes drifted by southwest winds. These may be thinner and smaller in size due to less snow available for transport. To simplify, I’d recommend looking for signs of wind drifting as soon as you find roughly 6 inches of recent snow. This will be more common above 6500 feet in elevation. Slabs should be easy to avoid with visual clues, as described in the Wind Slab problem description.
We removed Persistent Slab as a listed hazard on Monday. You can still find soft facets around a hard crust on many middle and upper-elevation slopes. Recent warm temperatures allowed these facets to gain strength. A supportable crust near the surface also adds strength to the snowpack by reducing the chances of collapsing the weak layer. In steep, high-consequence terrain, it is still worth digging to look for this structure, especially if you don’t find a supportable crust beneath your feet or machine.

Triggering these slabs can be dangerous on steep slopes above terrain traps. This hazard should be isolated to peaks and ridges above about 7000 feet near the crest of the Swan, Flathead, and Whitefish Ranges.\xa0'}, {'problem_type': ' Dry Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'likely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "While you can trigger sluffs of new snow on most steep slopes today, they'll be dangerous where they involve more than about 8\xa0 inches of new snow. So this hazard coincides with much of the most attractive terrain for riding today. These conditions will be more widespread at upper elevations, where more snow accumulated over the weekend.\xa0 Before committing to ascending or descending steep slopes,\xa0 assess whether you can escape triggered sluffs and where you might be carried if caught. Confined terrain like chutes and couloirs will make riding out of moving sluffs difficult. Otherwise harmless sluffs can have consequences if they knock you off your feet or machine above a terrain trap, like a gully or dense brush. If you see rollerballs fanning down a slope above you, move to terrain that's not below steep start zones. Slope cuts can be an effective tool in these conditions, so long as they're done in a spot that allows you to stay above the hazard and escape any triggered slides. Don't be too low, too slow, with nowhere to go.\xa0 On isolated slopes, the snow may form small, shallow slabs as it settles.\xa0 The terrain management remains the same.\xa0"}]
bottom_line: The weekend's new snow can pose a hazard on steep slopes, primarily at upper elevations. More new and drifted snow accumulated in this terrain, making triggered sluffs or small, thin slabs harder to escape. Avoid steep, confined slopes above gullies, dense brush, trees, rocks, and other features that can increase your chances of being buried or injured if you're surprised.
discussion:
And there was much rejoicing... A few inches of new snow above made for good if noisy riding Sunday.  Riders also reported easily-triggered sluffs that ran far on an underlying crust. Most were harmless because they didn't involve that much snow. I expect this hazard to persist today. The surface snow will likely settle as temperatures warm today, making for denser but slower-moving debris. In a few spots, the fresh snow may form small shallow slabs as it settles. These are unlikely to propagate far, and the risk reduction practices remain the same - avoid steep, confined slopes above terrain traps. 
Riders and remote weather stations reported calm to light winds Sunday, with little to no blowing snow. The exception was Aeneas Ridge, a perenially windy location that's often representative of conditions near ridge crests and summits. In this terrain, you may find some small, dense slabs on steep slopes that collect wind-blown snow - the tops of chutes, downwind of saddles and passes, and crossloaded behind rocks. If you encounter drifts that crack around your feet or machine, step back and assess the consequences of getting caught and carried. 
We've removed Persistent Slabs as a listed hazard. You can still find soft facets around a hard crust on many mid- and upper-elevation slopes. The facets are moist and rounding and we have no reports of whumpfing collapses since the warmup, and few reports of propagation in tests. A supportable surface crust makes it even less likely a person could collapse the facets and propagate a fracture. In steep, high-consequences terrain, it's still worth digging to look for and test this structure before committing.
A report Sunday from near Essex in the Flathead Range did describe a test that propagated on a more recently buried layer on an upper elevation, northeasterly slope. Another report from a similar slope near Pinnacle did not describe this layer. Bottom line: Any whumpfing collapse should stop you in your tracks. Digging to investigate the snowpack is almost always useful.

Dense snow, scalloped surfaces, and dunes are signs the wind has been at work. Cracking in the surface snow indicates instability, directing you to seek out sheltered terrain or a lower-angle slope.'}]
bottom_line: Atypical winds will continue to drift new snow in unusual places. Steer around fresh slabs to safely enjoy some much-needed powder. There remains a chance you could trigger an avalanche on a deeply buried weak layer on drier, northerly upper-elevation slopes.
discussion:
Snow is back in the forecast, and a welcomed cooling trend is expected today with northeast winds. As of 5 AM, snow accumulations are meager, with most mountain weather stations reporting several inches. However, snowfall totals will likely increase as you gain elevation, as blase and I observed yesterday while skiing 4 inches of new snow on the west side of the Continental Divide in the Skyland area. 
Cooler temperatures today will strengthen the recently warm, wet snowpack into a supportable crust, likely ending our concerns with wet snow instabilities. Yesterday (Saturday), observers (observation 1 and observation 2) around Whitefish Mountain Resort reported better-than-expected skiing in 1-2 inches of new snow above a supportable crust. 
Northeast winds are the wild card of our forecast. They often wreak havoc on the front side of WMR and the Swan front, while mostly unaffecting the narrow drainages of the Middle Fork. However, today’s winds are generally mild and short-lived compared to many of our recent Arctic intrusions. Today, locations that do not experience wind effects will remain at LOW danger. 
From January 28th through the 30th, numerous large avalanches ran naturally. Some slid on old, weak layers or stepped down to the ground. We are still looking for old crowns and avalanche debris to understand the full extent of this warm, wet storm. Check out our observation page for a catalog of reported avalanches.
Upcoming event: Flathead Avalanche Friends Fest, February 21-24. Beacons & Brews, Name That Tune/Name That Avalanche, Workshop Night, Hot Dog Days, and Ultimate Après.  Check out 4 days of events: flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

author: Rob Millspaugh
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Persistent Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'unlikely', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'northwest'], 'middle': [], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'Although unlikely, a rider could still trigger a large and destructive avalanche several feet deep in locations where a faceted layer is intact and weak. Following an abnormally warm week and widespread avalanche cycle, these locations will be limited to the highest slopes facing Northwest, through North, to East.\xa0 The snowpack may or may not give you clear signs of instability, such as collapsing, before avalanching.\xa0 In areas where you do not find a stout surface crust, dig down and look for weak, sugary snow near a crust formed in December.\xa0 Reduce your risk of causing an avalanche on this layer by choosing slopes without obvious trigger points. These include convexities, scoured slopes with a thin snow cover, and rock outcroppings.\xa0'}]
bottom_line: Generally safe conditions exist as the snowpack refreezes. Travel with a partner and carry a beacon, shovel, probe, as well as the knowledge to use them. At upper elevations, further reduce your risk by avoiding steep roll-overs or slopes peppered with rocks.
discussion:
Temperatures are cooling off. So too is the avalanche hazard. After a week of warm, upper-elevation weather stations finally fell below freezing last night.  Sites at middle elevations are right around freezing. We’ve dropped the wet slab problem to reflect decreasing meltwater in the snowpack. The persistent slab problem is still listed at upper elevations on shaded aspects to account for any slopes that were unaffected by this week’s warm-up. Recent observations of the Flathead crest suggest that even these high reaches saw warm temperatures and avalanche activity, though there is still some uncertainty due to challenging travel conditions.
Unfortunately, the forecasted snowfall for last night did not materialize as expected.  Noisy Basin, in the Swan Range, is the only station reporting an increase in SWE (0.3 inches), and possibly an inch or two of snow. With only a few inches of snowfall expected today, I don't anticipate there being enough fuel for new snow problems to affect the avalanche hazard.  IF you find more snow and wind than forecasted, you can expect thin and sensitive slabs of wind-drifted snow to form along ridges, saddles, and other prominent terrain features.  Consider moving to wind-sheltered, or less steep terrain if you find 8 or more inches of recent, wind-drifted snow. Note that winds may shift to northeasterly today along Highway 2, so you could find new slabs forming on slopes that are normally sheltered.
Although we are still collecting data, a widespread wet avalanche cycle happened in the past week. So far, the most dramatic and widespread slides appear to have occurred in the Swan Range near Condon. You can check out what has been reported in the avalanches tab on our observations page.
Connect with the community, learn about snow, and support our safety mission at the Flathead Avalanche Friends Fest, February 21-24.  Following this week's avalanche cycle, I'm more excited than ever to hear the US Geological  Survey talk about wet snow avalanches. Link to event page: flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

These include convexities, scoured slopes with a thin snow cover, and rock outcroppings. The snowpack may or may not give you signs, such as collapsing, before an avalanche occurs."}, {'problem_type': ' Wet Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "Today's warm temperatures continue the warming and wetting of the snowpack that began last weekend. Meltwater has reached and weakened a buried faceted crust, keeping the threat of wet snow avalanches through the day. Natural avalanche activity appears to have diminished but can not be ruled out. Triggering a large avalanche 2-4 feet deep remains possible on steep slopes, especially in locations without a supportable crust.\xa0 If you find an unsupportable crust on a steep slope, move to lower-angle terrain well away from the runouts of start zones. Mostly cloudy conditions are expected today, but if the sun makes an appearance, limit your time traveling on steep slopes receiving direct sun, especially this afternoon.\xa0 \xa0"}]
bottom_line: One last warm day means it is still possible for a rider to trigger a large wet avalanche. Limit the time you spend on or below steep slopes with an unsupportable crust. Choose low-angle terrain free of overhead hazards if you encounter mushy snow where your boards or machine sink below the surface.  There remains a chance you could trigger an avalanche on a deeply buried weak layer on drier, northerly upper-elevation slopes.
discussion:
Today should be the last unseasonably warm day before a cooler and snowier pattern arrives this weekend. Unusually warm weather arrived last weekend and has remained through the work week. An example is the Noisy Basin SNOTEL, where readings have remained above freezing since Saturday, January 27, or around 144 hours and counting. On several occasions, temperatures remained in the 40s F overnight! Unprecedented for late January?
Despite another night of above-freezing temperatures at all mid- and upper-elevation weather stations, the chance of a natural wet avalanche decreases slowly. Meltwater reached the December 22 buried faceted crust earlier in the week and caused mayhem. Now, the snowpack has adjusted chiefly to this initial rapid temperature change and influx of water. However, with a mushy snow surface in most locations, triggering a large destructive slide remains possible. Debris from a surface avalanche can trigger a buried weak layer, or this layer can be initiated by the weight of a rider. 
Yesterday (Thursday), I visited the popular sidecountry Skook Face just north of the WMR ski area. I looked closer at a wet slab that failed earlier in the week. Similar to the nearby Hellroaring Peak, much of the open, gladed terrain is not steep enough to slide. However, the skier's right side is plenty steep to slide and has slid in the past, surprising to many side country users. Compare these photos from yesterday and about 10 years ago in the media gallery below.
Although we are still collecting data, a widespread wet avalanche cycle happened in the past week. So far, the most dramatic and widespread slides appear to have occurred in the Swan Range near Condon. We have not visited the Flathead Range to catalog what happened in that extremely steep and rugged terrain. You can check out what has been reported in the avalanches tab on our observations page.

author: blase reardon
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Persistent Slab', 'problem_number': 2, 'likelihood': 'unlikely', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'northwest'], 'middle': [], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "This week's cycle of large and very large natural avalanches is clear evidence that hard slabs can break several feet deep - and hundreds to thousands of feet wide. The terrain where this hazard exists has steadily narrowed with four days of sun and unseasonably warm temperatures. However, this hazard lingers on high, shady slopes where slabs and weak layers remain dry. If you reach this terrain, beware of slopes with likely trigger points -\xa0 steep, with convex rollovers and variable snow depths. Audible collapses should send you scurrying for low angle slopes clear of overhead start zones.\xa0"}, {'problem_type': ' Wet Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "Triggering wet slab avalanches remains possible on steep slopes. These conditions may be most widespread where the snowpack has gradually warmed, such as shady, mid-elevation slopes and upper elevation slopes facing southeast and west. In this terrain, meltwater may still be percolating into weak layers buried around crusts that formed in December. The likelihood of natural wet snow avalanches is diminishing. However, careful route selection continues to be essential because of the potential size and consequences of slides like these.\xa0 Avoid traveling on or below steep start zones, particularly those under rock bands and receiving direct sun. Exit avalanche terrain early, particularly where your route will take you through the runouts of steep start zones.\xa0 Keep in mind that wet snow avalanche hazards can be unpredictable, particularly when inversions are present like they are this morning. Slopes with a refrozen snow surface are unlikely to avalanche, though you can't tell if these crusts exist at higher elevations. And a wet snow surface tends to give few clues to how meltwater is percolating and whether slabs are becoming unstable.\xa0"}]
bottom_line: While the likelihood of avalanches is gradually diminishing, the consequences of triggered and natural avalanches remain severe. An element of unpredictability makes careful travel essential. Limit your time in the runouts of steep start zones. Exit avalanche terrain early in the day.
discussion:
It's early for Groundhog Day jokes. And conditions aren't the same as yesterday (Wednesday). Natural and triggered avalanches are less likely, though the consequences remain severe. And unlike Bill Murray's weatherman character in the classic Groundhog Day film, we can't act with impunity, knowing we'll wake up just fine tomorrow morning. 
So if you head into the mountains to enjoy the spring-like conditions, maintain safe travel practices. Natural avalanches are unlikely on most slopes today. However, there's an element of unpredictability in the ongoing Wet Slab and Persistent Slab avalanche problems. How much and where is meltwater percolating into weak layers? Which high-elevation slopes retain an unstable persistent weak layer that can propagate a collapse long distances?
Careful route selection can help mitigate this unpredictability. The most important practice today is likely to minimize time spent in the runouts of steep start zones. That reduces your chances of getting hit by wet slides from terrain that's warming and becoming unstable. And, in shaded, upper elevation terrain,  of triggering a collapse that can release a large hard slab that releases on deeply buried weak layers. 
I've heard travel conditions described as "arduous" in the past few days. Engines overheating on icy roads, thin snow cover, postholing in wet snow, delicately stepping skis across bare patches like cats through a puddle. That said, there's probably some spring corn at mid elevations where the snow surface is refreezing at night. And maybe, on shaded, moderate-angles slopes at upper elevations, some surfy dry snow. 
We're still cataloging the natural avalanche cycle that occurred early this week. For now, it appears the largest slides and most widespread activity occurred in the Swan Range near Condon. The number and size of reported slides appears to trend down as you head north, though I suspect high peaks in the Flathead Range may have seen similar slides. Scroll through the avalanches tab on our observations page. The "visual" tab gives some good charts that allow a more wholistic view of what's happened, though we are still catching up entering events. Please let us know what you see if you get out today.

author: Guy Zoellner

The problem child is a layer of weak snow buried around a crust roughly 2-4 feet down.\xa0 If found, choose your terrain carefully.\xa0 Areas on the slope where the slab thins out are places where you could trigger a large Persistent Slab avalanche.\xa0 Travel around convexities and off of slopes connected to places where you find this combination. '}, {'problem_type': ' Wet Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'A natural avalanche cycle of Wet Slab avalanches continues.\xa0 Upper and middle elevations are living in above-freezing conditions. Meltwater has made its way to a weak layer buried 2-4 feet below the surface, causing it to become unstable. Steep slopes getting hit by the warm sun will be the most likely to avalanche. The weight of a skier or machine has the potential to tip the scale and cause a large and deadly avalanche.\xa0 Avoid steep slopes and give the runouts a wide margin because wet debris can travel surprisingly far distances.\xa0 Wet Slab avalanches are destructive and will fail without warning.'}]
bottom_line: Dangerous avalanche conditions still exist due to continued warm temperatures. Reports of very large natural avalanches are still being documented, proving the cycle is not quite over.  Give steep terrain plenty of room if taking advantage of the sunshine today.
discussion:
“Hazardous,” “scary,” “difficult,” and “ ... the worst” were all descriptors from people who traveled into the mountains yesterday (January 30th).  The unseasonably warm temperatures have caused significant changes to our snowpack and ease of travel.  A report from John F. Stevens Canyon describes knee-deep post-holing in isothermal snow. Meanwhile, I was in Noisey Basin on a very supportable and thick crust. 
The Flathead Range and Glacier National Park appear to have seen the most action with very large (destructive size 3) avalanches over the past 3 days.  Observers reported moist snow above a facet/crust combo that still lingers from late December.  These ranges seem to be warmer with a shallower and weaker snowpack.  Combine all of those to get a resulting widespread cycle of persistent and wet slab avalanches.  Many of these have run long distances and entrained nearly the entire season’s worth of (dismal) snow.  
For those of you brave enough to venture into the challenging conditions today, remember what is above you.  Upper elevation temperatures have been above freezing for several days, and the snowpack continues to be prime for wet snow avalanches.  Keep time spent under big terrain to a minimum.  Travel patterns that you are used to may take much longer than anticipated, or not be possible.

bottom_line: Very dangerous avalanche conditions exist due to unseasonably warm temperatures and a poor snowpack structure. Avalanches large enough to destroy a wood frame house have failed naturally over the last two days. The signs are clear: stay out of and out from underneath steep slopes.
discussion:
Highlights from yesterday are the large avalanches reported from the Swan Range and southern Glacier National Park. Here are some common characteristics of these avalanches:

 They ranged in size from Large (D2.5) to Historic (D4).
They are all recent (less than 48 hours old). 
These failed at upper elevations on west, southwest, and south aspects. The one exception was a Very Large slab avalanche that failed on a northwest aspect.
We suspect they all failed on facets buried around the Dec. 22 crust. 

The recently reported avalanches may be a teaser for more to come today. Overnight temperatures climbed into the high 30s and low 40s at 6000 and 7000-foot weather stations. Today marks the third day in a row with no refreeze at some upper elevation stations. Meltwater is moving through the snowpack and into buried persistent weak layers. This process is causing the majority of the recent natural avalanche activity.
The snowpack has not yet adjusted to water moving through it. While this process continues to climb higher in elevation, a fair amount of uncertainty exists. The extent of natural activity is hard to predict when dealing with multiple nights of no refreeze. Although I don’t expect a widespread avalanche cycle to occur today, I would expect more very large avalanches to fail naturally. Slopes above roughly 6000 feet will be the most likely to fail. Warm weather will begin to cause meltwater to flow through the snowpack on more northerly-facing aspects, which may cause this problem to become more widely distributed.
Avalanches today will run relatively long distances. The elevated ratings at the middle and lower elevations are primarily for overhead hazards.
The snowpack will become more stable once we get through this warming event. In the meantime, keep it simple.

bottom_line: Very dangerous avalanche conditions are developing thanks to unseasonably warm air and the potential for partially sunny skies today. Avoid traveling on or below all steep slopes; large natural wet snow avalanches are likely. Also rising is the danger posed by large to very large avalanches that release on deeply buried weak layers and run long distances. The debris from both hazards will be dense, heavy, and almost impossible to escape.
discussion:
While people often talk about a "January thaw" in the Flathead, this is something. Nearly all stations at mid elevations have recorded air temperatures above freezing for the past 48 hours, with 0.2 to 0.5 inches of rain. Freezing levels are forecast to climb higher today, possibly to near 10,000 feet. Although the rain looks over, it'll be replaced by partially clearing skies. So solar radiation will add to the energy that's producing meltwater on upper elevation, sunny slopes. 
The wet snow and meltwater are creating very dangerous avalanche conditions. Large to very large natural wet snow avalanches are likely on steep slopes at mid and upper elevations. Debris from these slides may reach low elevation runouts. Even slides that stop after short distances can involve large amounts of debris, as seen in the one observation we received Sunday. A rider reported multiple slides that hit or almost hit the snowmobile route across Red Meadow Pass in the central Whitefish Range. These released at mid elevations and only ran several hundred feet, but piled up more than enough debris to bury a person. I expect more of the same today, with most activity on upper elevation sunny aspects. The hazard is isolated at low elevations, because slides won't involve dangerous amounts of debris. There just as likely; I ran across two small debris piles on the Whitefish Trail near Lion Mountain Sunday. 
The danger posed by the lingering Persistent Slab avalanche problem is also increasing, as water percolates into the snowpack. The snowpack structure is weakest on sunny aspects at mid and upper elevations; that coincides with slopes where solar radiation will produce more meltwater today. This water will reach old, faceted snow above crusts soonest in terrain where these layers are closer to the surface. That describes the southeast corner of the forecast region - JF Stevens Canyon, Marias Pass, and the Skyland area, where observers have reported audible collapses and propagation in stabilty tests the past few days (Elk Mountain, 6996 Friday and Saturday, Challenge Friday). While cornices have been smaller than usual this season, there's still plenty of snow that can fail in these warm temperatures, producing debris that can tirgger deeply bured weak layers on slopes where a person might not. Leeward start zones near the crest of the Flathead, Swan, and Whitefish Ranges are prime candidates for these conditions. 
It's important to note that our confidence in today's forecast is moderate. We have no direct measures of the amount of meltwater at the snow surface or how deep it's percolating in the snowpack. The natural avalanche cycle may have peaked late last night. I wouldn't use that uncertainty to justify riding steep slopes.

bottom_line: Due to abnormally warm temperatures and rain, dangerous avalanche conditions have developed. Moist and wet surface snow may slide naturally or be easily triggered by a rider. The chance of triggering a large, destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer has increased. Today is a good day to practice safe travel techniques by sticking to low-angle slopes free of overhead hazards.
discussion:
Residual moisture from an Atmospheric River impacting western Washington and Oregon moved into the Flathead yesterday. Rising temperatures and increasing rain occurred as the day progressed. Noisy Basin in the Swan Range picked up 1 inch of water; most, if not all, fell in the form of rain. Stahl Peak in the northern Whitefish Range recorded one-half inch of water, also primarily as rain. Other locations appear to have received far less precipitation. Temperatures climbed overnight, and as of 0600, all stations are above freezing except for WMR and Link, which are both at 31F. Temperatures are forecast to climb throughout the day, with snow levels reaching 7500-8000 feet. Precipitation should be generally light and tapering off later today.
Rain on snow and warming temperatures will keep the avalanche danger elevated throughout the day. Yesterday (Saturday), I assume a wet loose avalanche cycle occurred at mid and possibly upper elevations favored by rainfall. The natural cycle should continue today and climb in elevation. Hour by hour, it will become easier for a rider to trigger a wet slide in moist or wet surface snow. 
Rain adds weight to the snowpack without adding strength. This added weight stresses buried weak layers, which have repeatedly shown this past week that they are still a concern. Multiple parties noted propagating pit results, natural avalanches (Example 1, Example 2), and collapsing. Debris from today's wet loose slides may be enough weight (stress) to tip the balance and trigger a large destructive avalanche.
Riders enjoyed dry snow and fun conditions above 6000 feet yesterday before the rain and warm temperatures affected the snow surface (Example1, Example 2). With dry weather and warm temperatures forecast for the next few days, riding dry snow will have to wait until the return of winter, perhaps later this week or next weekend.

bottom_line: If forecasted warm temperatures and rain occur this afternoon, dangerous avalanche conditions may develop. Dry snow becoming wet for the first time could initiate wet snow slides. The chance of triggering a large, destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer may increase. If you notice warming temperatures and rain, retreat to lower-angle terrain for a safer experience.
discussion:
An impressively warm and wet Atmospheric River moved into western Washington and Oregon yesterday, with over four inches of water expected in many locations. How much moisture makes it to the Flathead is a bit unknown due to a developing ridge of high pressure, which is partially blocking this wet air mass from impacting our area. There is a fair amount of uncertainty with today's forecast, but temperatures are expected to rise, and rainfall increase this afternoon. Rain may reach the middle elevations (5000 - 6500 feet) today, and wet, dry surface snow.  
If the forecast verifies, dangerous avalanche conditions may develop this afternoon. Despite recent warm weather, plenty of dry near-surface snow remains available to move downhill as wet loose slides. These slides can entrain substantial snow, adding to their mass and destructive capabilities. Debris from these slides could trigger a larger, more dangerous hard slab avalanche on buried weak layers. 
Temperatures continue to climb tonight and tomorrow, and rainfall increases. The avalanche danger will remain elevated throughout Sunday. Rain is forecast to reach the upper elevations, where plenty of dry snow remains. If today's forecasted avalanche hazard does not develop, we are confident these conditions will develop tomorrow.

bottom_line: You can still trigger a large and potentially deadly avalanche on weak snow buried deep in the snowpack.  Audible collapses are a clear signal to move off of, and out from under, steep slopes.  Further reduce your risk by limiting your travel above terrain traps like cliffs, gullies, or exposed rocks and trees. If your boards or machine sink into mushy snow, move to lower-angle terrain for a safer experience.
discussion:
The snowpack isn’t necessarily screaming at us, but it’s definitely still talking.
Yesterday (Thursday), a party in the central Whitefish Range reported a recent slab avalanche. This slide ran naturally and crossed a road where it piled debris over 8 feet deep. That’s plenty deep to bury a person and their machine. Meanwhile, a party in southern Glacier National Park encountered whumpfs during their ascent and adjusted their route to avoid steep, open terrain. Likewise, BNSF Avalanche Safety continues to document natural and triggered avalanches failing on depth hoar near the ground near Essex. These observations indicate that warm temperatures and incremental precipitation are still stressing buried weak layers.  When the snowpack is talking, it’s best to listen. Continue to evaluate terrain carefully where weak layers exist. If you’re uncertain where that is, dig into the snowpack to see what you’re standing on.
Generally, winds have been light and southwesterly with week. Outliers—like Aeneas Ridge in the Swan Range and Hornet in the Whitefish Range—have recorded sustained winds strong enough to drift snow. If you are traveling at upper elevations in an area favored by recent snow and wind, continue to evaluate terrain carefully. Look for signs of wind-affected snow, like a scalloped surface or dune-shaped feature along ridges, saddles, and other prominent terrain features. Find a less steep slope if wind-drifted snow cracks around your feet or machine. A small surface slide may potentially cause a larger, more destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer.

Limit your travel above terrain traps like cliffs, gullies, or exposed rocks and trees. If the surface snow is wet, move off of, and out from under, steep slopes.
discussion:
"Are we there yet?" [There being an unreactive persistent slab problem.]
 "No, not quite..."
Yesterday (Wednesday), A team from FAC traveled to the central Whitefish Range to de-rime the Link Mountain weather station. However, we quickly reprioritized when we encountered a recent avalanche and a booming collapse. Weak snow above the 12/22 crust was the culprit in both instances. This “bulls eye” data indicates that we’re not yet free and clear of the lingering persistent slab problem.
The largest collapse we encountered was on a southeast aspect at middle elevation.  Interestingly, when we dug a pit less than 100 feet away on an adjacent northeast slope, we found a much deeper snow pack and a less developed weak layer.  Also of note, we triggered this collapse just several steps after rerouting our uptrack away from a convex roll-over near the top of a slope. The collapse propagated a crack across the same convexity. Our takeaway:  it is still critical to choose ascents and descents that limit exposure on slopes where the weak and potentially unstable structure exists. 
Winds have generally been unimpressive during the first half of the week and will remain light and southwesterly. Aeneas Ridge in the Swan Range, however, is a notable exception; it's averaging over 20 mph. If you are traveling at upper elevations in an area favored by recent snow and wind, continue to evaluate terrain carefully. Look for signs of wind affected snow like a scalloped surface or dune-shaped feature along ridges saddles and other prominent terrain features. Find a less steep slope if wind-drifted snow cracks around your feet or machine. A small surface slide may have the potential to cause a larger, more destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer.

bottom_line: The danger of triggering slabs that fail on weak snow buried deep in the snowpack lingers. You can reduce your exposure to this hazard by sticking to planar or concave slopes with runouts free of rocks, brush, trees, and gullies. Triggering wet snow avalanches remains a hazard on steep slopes where the snow surface hasn't refrozen.
discussion:
Ch-ch-changes. Rain and wild swings in temperature are putting the snowpack in the region through some changes. In the short term, the quality of the riding is suffering. In the long run, the water and heat are reducing the distribution and sensitivity of a Persistent Slab avalanche problem that's a legacy from December. Cam's video below is a quick summary of the problem.
We're not in the clear yet, however, so it remains important to approach terrain cautiously. Evaluating terrain for potential consequences is more reliable than determing stability. Convex rolls - where slopes abruptly turn steeper - are likely trigger points. Runouts with dense alder and brush, cliffs, gullies, or roadbeds can increase your chances of being injured or fully buried. Leeward slopes that collect wind-drifted snow can harbor thin slabs that, once moving, can step down to more deeply buried weak layers. The terrain where you're least exposed to a Persistent Slab avalanche tends to be planar or concave slopes with clean runouts.
You can see the importance of terrain selection in the report on a fatal avalanche accident near Lookout Pass earlier this month. Events like that are devastating for the people and families involved. They can also act as mirrors that show alternate realities for our own habits and decisions. In Stevens Peak accident, the triggered slide failed in a snowpack with similar layers as ours, on a convex slope above a terrain trap. It highlights the importance of readily recognizing potentially dangerous terrain. That's a skill that develops with practice in conditions like we have now.

bottom_line: Warm temperatures and rain on snow will cause wet snow avalanches today. Below roughly 5500 feet, avoid traveling on steep slopes where you feel wet, manky snow, or see rain falling on snow. As you climb higher in elevation, it remains possible to trigger a slab avalanche failing on a weak layer buried 1 to 4 feet below the surface. Feeling the snowpack collapse or seeing recent avalanches are signs to give steep terrain a wide margin for error.
discussion:
Although the weather in the valley is purely uninspiring, snow is falling higher up, and the riding is pretty good. If you’re heading out today, here is what you may expect as you climb through the elevation bands.
Precipitation is falling as rain in the valley, and I would expect this to continue up to nearly 5500 feet. Rain on snow is a recipe for wet snow avalanches. These may fail as point releases, or as slabs due to an underlying persistent weak layer on a crust. Thick vegetation sticking through the snowpack reduces the areas where this is a problem. The locations to watch out for are steep road cuts, or where you see a steep snowfield that is free of alder and thick trees. The problematic locations are unfortunately the ones that look appealing to ride through. Although I expect most slides to be small, wet-moving debris is difficult to escape and nothing to tango with.
Above the rainline is where you will have to manage the Persistent Slab avalanche problem. Audible collapses and recent avalanches have occurred in the last few days. The culprit is faceted “sugary” snow buried on the 12/22 crust. If you haven’t been in the mountains for a while, you can find this crust at all elevations. The easy way to find this layer is by shoving your pole or probe into the snow and feeling for a crusty, hard layer. If you are on a snowmachine, you could likely find it by spinning your track deep into the snow and looking in the sidewall of the trench you just carved. Feel for weaker snow around a hard crust. The higher you climb, the deeper in the snowpack you have to look.
This facet/crust combination is proving problematic at lower and middle elevations. As you climb higher in elevation, the problem becomes more specific to aspects that face east, south, and west. This is a result of the sunny and warm weather we experienced in the middle of December. When snowfall returned in late December, it sat on the surface and faceted. 
Plan for this weak layer to be on all aspects if your travels take you closer to the divide. John F. Stevens Canyon, Skyland, Marias Pass, and Central Glacier National Park had a significantly shallower and weaker snowpack earlier in the season. Audible collapses and natural avalanches failed there during the past two storms.

bottom_line: Avoid steep, mid-elevation slopes above terrain traps, where you can trigger wet, loose snow avalanches with serious consequences. It also remains possible to trigger dangerous hard slabs that fail several feet deep. While the distribution of this hazard is unclear, it appears most acute on steep slopes that face northeast to southeast to southwest at all elevations.
discussion:
Driving home yesterday (Sunday) evening, it was 27 degrees and raining near Eureka. Near Stryker, it was 30 degrees and snowing. "Welcome to our upside-down world," remarked one of my climbing partners. "Colder means snow, warmer means rain, high means warm, and low means cold." 
Indeed, it is an inverted world, thanks to warm Pacific air overrunning the cold air entrenched in the valleys after our bitterly cold Arctic outbreak last week. That's made for some complicated conditions and a low confidence in today's forecast. My immediate uncertainty is the elevation range for the Loose Wet snow avalanche problem. It seems like the upper boundary of the above-freezing temperatures Sunday was between 6000 and 6500 feet, with the lower limit about 4500 feet. Valley temperatures flirted with freezing but mostly stayed colder. Many locations saw rain. It appears only the snow surface is wet there. My conclusion is that the possibility of triggering wet loose snow avalanches is more widespread at mid-elevations, though you may find exceptions at low elevations. 
A larger, more long-term uncertainty is the distribution of the lingering Persistent Slab avalanche problem. My best guess is that mid-elevation slopes in roughly the eastern half of the compass (NE-SE-SW) harbor the most well-developed Persistent Slab structure. And that this hazard is most widespread in the southeast corner of the forecast region. It's also present in the southern Whitefish Range. Elsewhere, it appears isolated. We don't, however, have many reports from upper-elevation terrain due to difficult travel conditions. So we can't write off this elevation band or colder aspects. 
What to do with this uncertainty? Test before committing to steep slopes. These tests can take many forms. My go-to's are slope-cutting small slopes with minimal consequences and conducting small and large column tests in a snow profile. Cam and Rob's video from last week shows how to do this on a snowmachine. For column tests, I'm looking for collapses or propagation in soft layers of facets near crusts. These will be mid-pack at mid and upper elevations and near the ground lower. Propagation and collapses indicate a Persistent Slab structure is present, and that's the key info, not the score. 
Here's the rationale for my guess about distribution: Over the past week, people have reported a series of small to large natural avalanches failing near the ground, on weak, faceted snow near a crust, many on northeast aspects. While we've had reports of audible collapses from all three zones, these have been most common in the southeastern corner of the forecast region, between Essex and Marias Pass (Essex area, JFS Canyon, Skyland/Challenge area). The snowpack was shallowest in this region prior to January's storms. Last week's storms seem to have produced several large to very large natural hard slab avalanches in the Flathead Range; near Snowslip and in JFS Canyon, these look to have failed midpack, below the storm snow, at 6500 feet or above.

bottom_line: Triggering a large destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer remains possible. Warm temperatures will keep the threat of wet slides at all elevations. Variable surface conditions could make travel difficult.
discussion:
Thursday's bitterly cold temperatures preserved Wednesday's 1-2 feet of low-density snow. Unfortunately, temperatures at mid and upper elevations began rising Friday and continued overnight. Saturday, all mid-elevation and a couple of upper-elevation stations climbed above freezing, while valley cold pools limited temperatures to the mid-20s. A freezing rain crust was noted in Essex and Ferndale on Saturday morning, with mixed precipitation continuing through the day. This morning (Sunday), temperatures remain mild at mid and upper-elevation locations, with Noisy Basin above freezing since 0500 Saturday. The valley's cold pool may get mixed out, with temperatures potentially climbing above freezing which would end the freezing rain concerns.
Today (Sunday), expect to find a thin, breakable crust on many surfaces above the valley cold pool, which may be capped with a thin layer of fresh snow. This crust could break down as temperatures warm through the day. Beneath the crust, there remains 1-2 feet of dry snow, which was the cause of yesterday's difficult travel and excessive skin glopping. Natural wet loose activity should be limited to locations where the surface stayed dry. However, with last night's weak freeze, the possibility of a rider-triggered slide remains at all locations. If so, we expect to see rollerballs and wet loose activity on steep slopes at low elevations, such as cutbanks or road cuts.  
A party in the Skyland area experienced multiple audible collapses in sheltered and wind-affected terrain with shooting cracks. The snowpack in this area is thin and possibly the weakest of any location in the Flathead. Below the warm air, several parties noted substantial cracking and collapsing in areas harboring cold, dry snow capped by a freezing rain crust. 
Despite increasing winds today, we have removed wind slab from the problem list. We assume the moist, wet, or frozen surface snow is limiting transport. If you do reach the upper elevations, keep an eye out for thin slabs on typical leeward aspects. 
Observations from Saturday were not inspiring. FAC visited Marion Lake in the Essex area, where we encountered six experienced groups who lamented how bad the riding was. Most turned around early to limit to limit their exposure to injury. Nearby, a veteran observer mentioned that this snow was some of the worst glopping skinning he had ever experienced. Another seasoned user's take-home message from his Essex tour was that the climb and descent were very difficult and the skiing was horrendous.  Four observations from the southern Whitefish Range mentioned warm, moist, and heavy surface snow (Observation 1, Ob 2, Ob 3, Ob 4). Observations like these make you want to stay home and cheer on the Buffalo Bills this afternoon.

bottom_line: Today's increasing temperatures may lead to a rising avalanche hazard. Surface slabs and loose snow may be easier to trigger than over the past few days. Small surface slides can cause a larger, more destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer. When in doubt, default to lower-angle terrain free of overhead hazards.
discussion:
Temperatures this morning are 20-30 degrees warmer than this time yesterday. A rapid increase in temperature is a red flag as this can cause rapid settlement in the recent snow and increase sensitivity. Furthermore, a couple vigilant observers already reported freezing rain at low elevations this morning. Rain on snow is a surefire way to cause an avalanche, but today’s incoming precipitation should be minimal.  Regardless, be on the lookout for signs that the snow surface is becoming wet like roller balls and pinwheels.  There’s a lot of recent snow out there and it could get messy as it warms up.
More tolerable temperatures also allowed folks to return to the mountains yesterday (Friday). A handful of observations continued to document widespread soft slab activity during peak snowfall Wednesday evening. They did not note any lingering signs of instability.
BNSF Avalanche Safety returned to the alpine as well and offered this synopsis of the Persistent Slab problem from their program area in John F Stevens Canyon:

Below 5500-6000’, where there was little to no snow before the New Year, there is a layer of depth hoar at the base of the snowpack that has been buried by 2-3’ of snow from the January storms. Ski penetration is often still to the ground.
Above 6000’, where a more developed snowpack exists, the 2-4’ of snow from the January storms is resting on top of a weak layer of facets that rests on top of a stout melt-freeze crust from late-December.

They also added “Both weak layers are producing audible collapses and shooting cracks. I lost count of the number of audible collapses, but I have not experienced that many in one day for quite some time.”

bottom_line: You can still trigger a large avalanche in both wind-affected and sheltered terrain. Small slides could cause a larger, more destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer. Manage your sluff on steep slopes with loose surface snow, and opt for low-angle terrain if the snow cracks and collapses under your feet or machine.
discussion:
It’s been a good fight, but only one can prevail in the cage match between warm and cold air masses. As we enter the weekend, an incoming warm front will choke out any remaining arctic air. Currently, cold air is still entrenched in the Middle Fork and North Forks of the Flathead, where it’s -20 to -10 degrees Fahrenheit, but it will soon be on its way out.  
Probably the biggest winners in this rumble were people who enjoy powder snow. Wednesday’s overrunning event produced 1-2 feet of low-density snowfall throughout the advisory area. Yesterday (Thursday), Cam and I visited the Flathead Range, where we found deep blower snow on all aspects and elevations. We also used great visibility to catalog recent avalanches along the crest. Many small to large (D1-D2) sluffs and soft slabs had run mid-storm.  We also noted several very large (D3) avalanches that had run during the last two weeks. Likewise, BNSF Avalanche Safety reported a widespread soft slab cycle late Wednesday evening in John F Stevens Canyon. 
Although natural activity has tapered off, a rider can still trigger a slab avalanche in wind-affected and sheltered terrain. With new snow and tolerable temperatures, the hype is high to get out of the house and into the mountains. Take a conservative mindset as you return to the slopes.

bottom_line: Dangerous avalanche conditions have developed due to abundant snowfall and blustery north and east winds, creating multiple avalanche problems. You could trigger a slab avalanche in both wind-affected and sheltered terrain. Surface slides may initiate an avalanche on a buried weak layer. Where the snow surface remains loose, you must manage your sluff on steep slopes.
discussion:
Cool temperatures and Pacific moisture combined to deposit bountiful low-density snow across the area Wednesday. Noisy Basin received 1.4 inches of SWE, approximately twice what most other locations picked up. Snow totals are notoriously difficult to determine with cold smoke-type snow, but most areas picked up a foot or more, with Tunnel Ridge recording 20+ inches. As the temperatures plummeted, the depth of new snow increased substantially. An example of this is Shed 11 in John F Stevens Canyon recording over 6" new snow in 2 hours at -2F. Snowfall generally ended well before midnight. 
Arctic air and north through east winds returned mid-day at most locations. Wind speeds increased overnight, with moderate to strong gusts recorded on the valley floor and at upper elevations. The cool air mass has dropped temperatures below zero this morning at all mid and upper-elevation stations. 
Due to the large amount of new snow and atypical winds, we have uncertainty with today's avalanche problems. Travel with a conservative mindset, staying well away from overhead hazards. Choosing to enjoy the deep powder on low-angle slopes is a sure-fire way to make the most of today's amazing snow.
Yesterday (Wednesday), Rob and Cam visited the Swan Range just south of Swan Lake. They found a foot-thick soft slab on top of the 12/22 crust, which cracked and collapsed on a layer of weak snow resting atop the crust at low elevations. Utilizing snowmachines, they produced shooting cracks 100 feet long on the same weak layer. Blase and Jeff visited the terrain north and west of WMR. They found the persistent slab structure on a southwest aspect at 6600 feet. It was not reactive in tests, though the weak layer was obvious. They triggered one small soft slab avalanche that involved just the new snow, though it seemed to propagate widely. BNSF snow safety noted severe wind loading with fresh wind slabs forming on the Canyon floor in John F Stevens Canyon

bottom_line: Dangerous avalanche conditions have developed due to abundant snowfall and blustery north and east winds, creating multiple avalanche problems. You could trigger a slab avalanche in both wind-affected and sheltered terrain. Surface slides may initiate an avalanche on a buried weak layer. Where the snow surface remains loose, you must manage your sluff on steep slopes.
discussion:
Cool temperatures and Pacific moisture combined to deposit bountiful low-density snow across the area Wednesday. Noisy Basin received 1.4 inches of SWE, approximately twice what most other locations picked up. Snow totals are notoriously difficult to determine with cold smoke-type snow, but most areas picked up a foot or more, with Tunnel Ridge recording 20+ inches. As the temperatures plummeted, the depth of new snow increased substantially. An example of this is Shed 11 in John F Stevens Canyon recording over 6" new snow in 2 hours at -2F. Snowfall generally ended well before midnight. 
Arctic air and north through east winds returned mid-day at most locations. Wind speeds increased overnight, with moderate to strong gusts recorded on the valley floor and at upper elevations. The cool air mass has dropped temperatures below zero this morning at all mid and upper-elevation stations. 
Due to the large amount of new snow and atypical winds, we have uncertainty with today's avalanche problems. Travel with a conservative mindset, staying well away from overhead hazards. Choosing to enjoy the deep powder on low-angle slopes is a sure-fire way to make the most of today's amazing snow.
Yesterday (Wednesday), Rob and Cam visited the Swan Range just south of Swan Lake. They found a foot-thick soft slab on top of the 12/22 crust, which cracked and collapsed on a layer of weak snow resting atop the crust at low elevations. Utilizing snowmachines, they produced shooting cracks 100 feet long on the same weak layer. Blase and Jeff visited the terrain north and west of WMR. They found the persistent slab structure on a southwest aspect at 6600 feet. It was not reactive in tests, though the weak layer was obvious. They triggered one small soft slab avalanche that involved just the new snow, though it seemed to propagate widely. BNSF snow safety noted severe wind loading with fresh wind slabs forming on the Canyon floor in John F Stevens Canyon

author: blase reardon
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Persistent Slab', 'problem_number': 2, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "Today, it's also possible to trigger thick, dangerous hard slabs that break below the new snow, on buried weak layers near crusts. We've found the most distinct persistent slab structure on mid-elevation slopes in the southern Whitefish Range and in the Swan Range. We're not clear how widely it's distributed beyond that.\xa0 On steep cutbanks and road cuts at low elevations, you may be able to trigger small, thin slabs on weak snow near the ground.\xa0 The added weight of today's storm snow may increase the sensitivity of these hard slabs. Natural and triggered slides in the new snow may also step down to these deeper failure planes, creating slides that a person is unlikely to survive. This hazard is another reason to avoid being on or under steep slopes today.\xa0"}, {'problem_type': ' Storm Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'likely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "Natural and triggered soft slab avalanches will become more likely - and larger - as low-density snow accumulates today. These slabs will be dangerous where they're more than about 8 inches thick or release above terrain traps.\xa0 They'll break more easily and wider where winds have preferentially deposited the storm snow. Where the new snow isn't cohesive enough to form slabs, sluffs that entrain scary amounts of snow are possible.\xa0 Careful and cautious routefinding is the key to enjoying the new snow while reducing your risk of getting caught or buried in an avalanche of new snow today.
