Pick lines that have runouts free of alder thickets, gullies, and rocks to reduce the potential consequences if your caught. Enter slopes high or from the side, where you can escape or let debris go by if a slope releases. Ride one at a time so you have the best chances of cleaning up surprises or mistakes. Clear signs of increasing danger are cracking in the new snow and fresh avalanches.\xa0"}]
bottom_line: As new snow accumulates today, the likelihood and size of triggered and natural soft slab avalanches will rise. Adding to the danger today is the possibility of triggering hard slabs that break deeper in the snowpack. Enjoy the new snow - and moderate temperatures! - by sticking to low angle slopes that aren't below steep start zones or above terrain traps. As the day progresses - and the danger rises - resist the temptation to chance it on consequential terrain.
discussion:
Don't be fooled by the fluff today. A forecast for a foot or more of 5% density snow - at tolerable temperatures! - will likely leave lots of people calling in sick to work or putting off promised jobs. However, the avalanche danger will rise as the day progresses and new snow accumulates. Low-density snow can be surprisingly cohesive (because of the large dendritic grains), particularly where the surface is slightly stiffened by the wind. And the fluff is easily entrained, making triggered or natural slides surprisingly dangerous. Ensure you can enjoy the new snow as it piles up this afternoon - and tomorrow, when the sun comes out - by paying careful attention to slope angles, runouts and safe travel practices today.
Also, don't be fooled by the fluff into thinking that avalanches involving new snow are the only hazard today. Lurking below the new snow are stubborn hard slabs that can be triggered by a person on steep slopes. These can release 18 to 40 inches deep on weak snow near crusts or the ground. This hazard exists primarily at mid and upper elevations; it's isolated at lower elevations due to the thin snowpack and very prominent anchors and surface roughness. We've found the structure that's most conducive to human triggering at mid-elevations in the southern Whitefish Range. Evidence that it a similar structure exists in the other zones  includes triggered and remote slides in the Lost Johnny and Six Mile areas of the Swan Range. and John F. Stevens Canyon in the Flathead zone. The uncertainty about the distribution of these weak structures requires snow profiles and tests to determine if it's present where you're riding. But that takes time away from powder riding; stick to low-angled terrain and make the most of your day. 
There's some disagreement in the weather models this morning regarding snow totals. That's common in overrunning events like these. Snow falling at high rates - more than an inch an hour - will make conditions dangerous sooner.

bottom_line: Facet/crust combinations or surface hoar buried 1-3 feet deep may still pose a danger today. If triggered, these avalanches will be large and break wider than you expect. Avoid convex rollovers and parts of the slope where the slab thins.
discussion: A welcome reprieve from the Arctic blast comes today in the form of increased temperatures and light winds. As we all crawl out from behind our woodstoves and blankets, here are a few things to keep in mind. Observations from the last five days have been minimal. 24+ inches of low-density snow and winds gusting in the 80s dramatically changed the snowpack from a week ago. We have a significant storm on the horizon tomorrow.   Before the cold front, there was ample snow available for transport. Strong winds from varying directions created thick slabs on atypical slopes. We have received reports of natural avalanches in all three mountain ranges. Despite the lack of observations, slabs of wind-drifted snow have had ample time to gain strength. A hardy observer found unreactive wind slabs in the Flathead Range yesterday(observation).  Don’t forget the structure before the last storm. We had weeks of high and dry conditions with crusts forming on many aspects. Facets formed around these crusts and/or surface hoar grew above them. The bountiful snow we received in early January buried all of it. Persistent Slab avalanches failing on these layers were reported within the last week (observation).   As the next storm arrives tonight, be aware of what the new snow is falling on. Clear and cold temperatures have already begun to weaken the snow surface. North and northeast-facing slopes were scoured, and south-to-southwest-facing slopes have old hard wind slabs. Both could become a bed surfaces for the new snow to slide on. Continued observations from the field are very much appreciated.

bottom_line: It is possible to trigger a large avalanche that fails on a weak layer buried 1-3 feet below the surface. Recent northerly winds have drifted snow into thick, hard slabs in unusual locations. Both problems may not give you feedback until it is too late. Seek out terrain protected from the wind and avoid common trigger points.
discussion:
Although the danger rating is Moderate, the avalanche conditions remain tricky. The likelihood of triggering a large slab avalanche is becoming less, yet still possible. Cold arctic air remains in place and can turn a simple mishap into a life-threatening situation.
The largest uncertainty in today’s avalanche problem rests with the Persistent Slab problem. Arctic air has kept many users out of the backcountry, reducing the number of observations. Due to the higher-than-usual uncertainty, the safest bet is to treat all slopes above 5000 feet as potential suspects for having these weak layers. The picture is not totally clear yet with this problem, but here is what we know:

 Reported avalanche activity failing on persistent weak layers has occurred on east-facing aspects between 5000-7000 feet (see visual). Additionally, north of the border, a rider remotely triggered two large avalanches failing on a similar weak layer. These occurred on January 10th (observation).
Before the storm, facet/crusts and weak surface snow were documented on most aspects above 5000 feet. The facet/crust appeared weakest and most noticeable at middle elevations (photo of facet/crust before the storm).
The weak layer is buried 1-3+ feet down from the surface. It will be buried deeper where winds have drifted more snow. You will be able to feel it with your probe or pole in areas where it does exist.
The snowpack is significantly thinner the closer you are to the continental divide. In that terrain, look for large facets near the ground (observation, avalanche observation).

bottom_line: You could trigger a large avalanche on a weak layer buried 1-3 feet below the surface. Winds have drifted snow into thick, hard slabs in unusual locations. Both of these problems may not give you feedback before they fail. Continued cold conditions dictate a short, simple field plan.
discussion:
The current deep pool of cold air arrived in our advisory area at John F. Stevens Canyon on Wednesday, the 10th. Numerous weather highlights occurred during these five days, including air temperature readings of -49F in Polebridge and -48F at Marias Pass, along with impressive wind chills of -70F at Snowslip and Tunnel Ridge and -68F at the summit of the WMR ski area. To add insult to injury, Snowslip recorded air temperatures of -30F or greater for 45 consecutive hours, with Flattop below -40F for 16 consecutive hours.
Fun fact: It reached -40F in Ferndale Saturday morning, where a frequent contributor reminded us that  -40F = -40C
Yesterday (Saturday), winds shifted to the south or west at most mid and upper-elevation stations, ushering in a warmer air mass and increasing temperatures overnight. Readings this morning at mid and upper elevations are generally 15-25 degrees warmer than this time yesterday. Unfortunately, cold air remains entrenched in the valley floor, where warming will be slower to occur.
The cold temperatures have slowed the strengthening process with recently formed wind slabs and buried weak layers. Wind slabs encountered today should generally be of the hard variety and supportable to you or your machine weight. Hard slabs are unpredictable, and I would attempt to avoid them whenever possible. Our Persistent Slab problem remains a bit of a mystery due to a lack of observations. With rising temperatures, FAC staff hopes to view various avalanche terrain in the coming days to determine how the buried weak layers reacted to the recent loading event. We also plan on digging into the snow and seeing how these weak layers fare in snowpack tests.
Understandably, observations have been scarce these past few days. Saturday, the WMR ski patrol noted slabs in unusual places. A polar explorer visited southern Glacier Park just east of Marias Pass and found numerous signs of instability, including shooting cracks and substantial wind loading. On Friday, BNSF snow safety noted multiple wind slab avalanches and at least one larger Persistent Slab in John F. Stevens Canyon.
Unfortunately, an avalanche fatality occurred Thursday in the Idaho Panhandle National Forest. A preliminary report of the incident can be viewed here.

bottom_line: Keep your plan simple to stay safe during extreme and dangerous cold. You might find sensitive slabs of wind-drifted snow in unusual locations. A slide failing on a buried weak layer may result in a larger, more destructive avalanche.
discussion:
Things break when it’s this cold. Many weather stations stopped reporting overnight. The ones still online are recording temperatures of -40 to -20 degrees Fahrenheit. Winds seem to have tapered overnight, which will technically decrease the wind chill hazard. Regardless, I still plan to limit my time outside warm buildings and vehicles today.
A break in the wind and snowfall will also start a trend of decreasing avalanche hazard.  However, we’ll begin this trend gradually. Cold temperatures can slow the healing of instabilities in the snowpack. Limited observations and weather data increase uncertainty.
We added Persistent Slab to our list of problems yesterday (Friday). With the precipitation ending, our attention focuses on weak layers below the surface. We currently have limited information on the exact distribution of this problem. Here’s what we’ve seen so far:

Sunday, 1/7: An experienced rider remotely triggered slides on Six Mile's 12/22 crust/facet combination. I returned to the same area on Wednesday and had propagating test results in my snowpit.
Monday, 1/8: In Lost Johnny, Cam found facets resting on a crust that initiated a collapse and triggered a small 2-foot-thick, soft slab.
Monday, 1/8: Blase found widely propagating natural avalanches and unstable snowpit test results on a layer of facets in the southern Whitefish Range.
Thursday, 1/11: Cam and Mark visited the site of a Persistent Slab avalanche in a popular side-country location east of the WMR ski area. This slide was near a well-used skin trail and occurred either naturally or remotely triggered. The crown was up to 3 feet thick and failed on a thin layer of facets just above the 12/22 crust.
1/10-1/12: BNSF Avalanche Safety reported avalanches (D1-D2) failing on faceted snow near the ground (1/10, 1/11, 1/12)

bottom_line: Slabs of wind-drifted snow reactive to the weight of a rider may be found in unusual locations. You could trigger a slide on a buried weak layer, resulting in a larger, more destructive avalanche. Dangerously cold conditions require a simple, straightforward field plan.
discussion:
Bitterly cold arctic air has settled over the Flathead Valley. Ambient air temperature readings are generally 20 to 30 below zero, with several stations at negative 38! Snowslip stopped updating at 2300 last night with a negative 38 reading and is probably colder than that now. Winds shifted to the north or east yesterday, producing dangerously cold wind chill readings. The National Weather Service has issued a wind-chill warning until 1100 on Saturday. Tunnel Ridge in the Flathead is the outlier station, where the wind remains southerly.
Snow and precipitation totals for the past 24 hours are minimal except for Noisy Basin in the Swan Range, where 10 inches of settled snow accumulated with 0.6 inches of water. If measured on a 24-hour storm board, this would equate to 6% density. However, due to settlement, we assume more snow than 10 inches fell, making the density even less. An observer in Ferndale reported 9.5 inches new with a mere 0.31 inches of water. This registers around 3% density, or as he stated, "blower indeed." Ferndale fluff?
Thursday's atypical winds were forming wind slabs on my tour yesterday in the southern Whitefish Range at all elevations. Our exit was challenging, with numerous fresh drifts and fallen tree branches adding a bit of excitement to warm us up. BNSF snow safety reported wind slabs forming at all John F. Stevens Canyon elevations. North and east winds are known for haphazard destruction. They often ruin the snow surface on the west side of the Swan Range and the southern aspects of WMR, while the narrow valleys of the Middle Fork and sheltered locations on the east side of the Swan go unscathed. Often, they do as much, if not more, damage at lower elevations in the valley floor.
We have added Persistent Slab to our list of problems this morning. We initially discussed including this problem last weekend, but for clear messaging, we utilized wind and storm slab to cover this past week's stormy weather. With the precipitation ending, our attention focuses on weak layers below the surface. Yesterday (Thursday), Cam and I visited the site of a Persistent Slab avalanche in a popular side-country location east of the WMR ski area. This slide was near a well-used skin trail and occurred either naturally or remotely triggered. The crown was up to 3 feet thick and failed on a thin layer of facets just above the 12/22 crust. On Six Mile in the Swan Wednesday, Rob noted propagation in snowpit tests on buried facets and surface hoar. In Lost Johnny Monday, Cam found facets resting on a crust that initiated a collapse and triggered a small 2-foot-thick, soft slab. An experienced rider remotely triggered slides on the 12/22 crust/facet combination Sunday on Six Mile.

bottom_line: Complex and dangerous avalanche conditions are developing due to changing weather. Triggering an avalanche failing 1 to 3 feet deep is likely. Avalanches may fail naturally and run long distances. Plan a simple day as extreme cold will complicate any rescue effort.
discussion:
“Wind is the architect of avalanches” – Wilhelm Paulcke, circa 1926
There is a new architect in town as arctic air sets into the advisory area. The well-advertised arctic front arrived at Marias Pass yesterday (Wednesday) morning and made its way down into the Flathead Valley.  Currently, it’s stalled out somewhere between Kalispell and Bigfork but should resume its progress south today. As the front passes, many stations have seen winds shift to northeasterly.  Some, like at WMR, are holding out with prevailing southwesterlies.  Others, like Aeneas Ridge in the Swan Range, or Hornet in the northern Whitefish Range are eerily calm. Ultimately, I’d expect winds to start forming thin, reactive slabs on slopes where you normally wouldn’t find them. Old, stubborn slabs likely remain on typical lees, particularly at upper elevations.  This was case in John F. Stevens Canyon yesterday.
Storm snow is still stacking up throughout the advisory area. We’ve expanded the storm slab problem to lower elevations for all zones. Stahl Peak in the northern Whitefish Range picked up over 1.0 of SWE in the last 24 hours and nearly 12 inches of snow.  This brings the total SWE to over 4.0 inches for this location since January 4th.  Default to low-terrain if you’re traveling in this area.
Extreme cold is a hazard in its own right, and a good reason to plan a simple day with wide margins for safety.  Stay warm!

bottom_line: New snow and strong winds have created dangerous avalanche conditions. Triggering an avalanche failing 1 to 3 feet deep is likely. Avalanches may fail naturally and run long distances. Enjoy the fresh snow today by sticking to low-angled terrain that is free of overhead hazards.
discussion:
Multiple small to large (D1-D2) avalanches have been reported since this past weekend. Slab avalanche activity has occurred on most aspects above 6000 feet. Yesterday (Tuesday), Ski Patrol at Whitefish Mountain Resort reported triggering slides that ranged from 5 to 30 inches thick. Some avalanches were remotely triggered and propagated wide distances. Avalanches also failed as low as 5300 feet in elevation, which is far lower than we have experienced this season.
Wind speeds yesterday made up for the last few weeks of calm conditions. Tunnel Ridge in the Flathead Range clocked a gust of 103 mph! Average wind speeds ranged from 15 to 30 mph throughout the forecast area. These speeds are prime for drifting new and recent snow into hard slabs. Winds will shift to the east today, and drift snow onto aspects that typically don’t get loaded by the wind. Pay close attention to surface clues and active wind transport.
Precipitation totals are starting to stack up. Below are the totals since January 4th:

Stahl (northern Whitefish Range): 3 inches of water
Flattop (central Glacier National Park): 2.8 inches of water
Noisy Basin (Swan Range): 3.5 inches of water

Except for yesterday, most of the snow has been low-density. This led to impressive snow totals throughout the forecast area. Temperatures crept up into the mid-twenties at 6000 feet yesterday. Rising temperatures and denser snow caused some remote weather stations to settle faster than snow accumulated. This rapid settlement and upside-down new snow may have formed the slab that we were missing earlier in the storm. Pay close attention to cracking and collapsing in the snow. 
An arctic air mass will drop temperatures to below zero tonight. Frigid temperatures increase the consequences of a small injury or broken equipment. The avalanche conditions are dangerous, and so is the weather. Plan short and simple days.

bottom_line: An Avalanche Watch is in effect. Dangerous avalanche conditions exist. Strong winds combined with new snow will lead to widespread areas of unstable snow. Avalanches will be larger and more dangerous where winds transport snow into thick slabs. Stick to lower-angled slopes that are protected from the wind.
discussion:
We issued an Avalanche Watch at 6:00 PM on Monday. Forecasted snow totals and strong winds will increase the avalanche danger throughout the day. It is likely to trigger a slab avalanche involving new and wind-drifted snow. 
This morning we will see increased snowfall rates in all ranges as the cold front passes through. Convective snow showers this afternoon will lead to bands of heavy snowfall. These bands may be spotty, which could make one drainage more dangerous than another. It will be up to you to assess conditions as you go. Look at each slope throughout the day with a fresh set of eyes. Pay close attention to snowfall rates and to areas where winds are drifting snow. 
This weekend’s storm deposited 1 to 3 feet of new snow across the forecast region. Recent avalanche activity has primarily consisted of soft slabs and loose dry avalanches. Blase did find a few natural hard slabs in the northern Whitefish Range on Sunday. These slabs failed at the base of cliffs where sluff debris piled up deep. Human-triggered slab avalanches occurred as recently as yesterday (Monday). New snow and strong winds today will spur another round of natural or human-triggered avalanches.

bottom_line: Up to 2 feet of new snow has fallen in 48 hours. Natural and human-triggered avalanches are expected.  Keep your powder fever in check today by evaluating steep slopes, especially if you see wind-drifted snow or shooting cracks around your skis or machine.
discussion:
“Top 5 deepest days in the Flathead.” “We were SO pleasantly surprised.” “Super terrific S2 storm snow.” “The storm finally delivers!”  These were descriptions captured from observations on Sunday. 
The storm favored the Whitefish, the Swan, and the Flathead Ranges, where more than an inch of water fell. Due to the cool temperatures, the storm snow fell more like cold smoke or champagne powder than we usually see in Northwest Montana. Due to the low density, snowfall totals are difficult to capture accurately, but observers report 1-2 feet or more. The snow density was less than 10% in most locations and probably closer to 5%. This is a welcomed contrast from the last two weeks of mostly dry, inversion-dominated weather. 
Natural and human-triggered storm slabs and loose dry avalanches were reported from various aspects and elevations (Observation 1, Observation 2, Observation 3). Today, we expect a temporary break in the active storm pattern, with increasing southwesterly winds anticipated to transport the low-density snow. Given that much of our region has a variety of crusts now buried under the new storm snow, a building wind slab problem will likely be found on east-north-west aspects.
In some sheltered locations, you may encounter sluffing. Remember that if you venture into steep slopes, even a small slide may be consequential if this sluff catches you off guard and pushes you into trees, rocks, cliffs, or other hard objects. Low elevation hazards are still plentiful, as our snowpack remains thin below 6000 feet in many locations.

bottom_line:
Up to a foot of snow fell overnight, with more on tap for today. The avalanche danger may be higher and more widespread than it has been in quite a while. You could trigger slides from gentler terrain below, above, and to the sides of steep slopes. Cracking beneath your skis or machine indicates an instability directing you to lower-angle terrain.
The danger will be lower in areas that only received a few inches of snow, like John F. Stevens Canyon.
discussion:
The third time, a charm! The Thursday and Friday storm systems were generally disappointing, but the disturbance that moved into our area mid-day Saturday has delivered. As of 0500 this morning, settled new snow depths at mid-elevation stations are around 9-12 inches in the Whitefish Range, the Swan Range, the Flathead Range, and central Glacier Park. Snow water equivalent (SWE) readings range from 0.7 to 1.0 inches.  Unfortunately, the Highway 2 corridor from John F. Stevens Canyon to Marias Pass only picked up a measly couple of inches. 
Winds have been relatively light over the past 24 hours, except for Aeneas Ridge, where average wind speeds have been 30 mph, well above the minimum threshold of 16 mph required for snow transport. Winds have shifted to the north or east at multiple stations as a weak arctic intrusion moved into the Flathead overnight. This cold air will interact with the relatively warmer, moist air from the Idaho Panhandle, producing light snow throughout the day. Where atypical winds occur, snow will drift onto westerly and southerly aspects. 
The new snow falls on 4 to 12 inches of soft snow, capping a crust. Any slides that occur today should entrain most, if not all, of the new snow above the crust. Observations report the near-surface crust to be relatively strong, but in more extensive terrain, slide debris may break through this crust into weak, faceted snow and produce a larger slide. 
Before the recent stormy period, an extensive dry snap formed weak, faceted snow or surface hoar on the surface in various locations. Observations over the past few days report mostly stable conditions with minimal instability (Observation, Observation, Observation). However, we remain concerned that today's slabs may be resting on this weak snow. On these slopes, slides may behave more like a Persistent Slab avalanche and could be triggered remotely from relatively flat terrain above, below, and to the sides of steep slopes. Resulting avalanches may break wider and higher than expected.

author: Rob Millspaugh

Due to the underlying weak layer, slabs may be triggered remotely or from a distance. If you suddenly notice your boards or machine not sinking as deeply into the new snow, you have stumbled onto a slab. Use hand pits or small inconsequential slopes to test how well the fresh snow bonds to the old surface. Cracking in the snow surface indicates an unstable slab and is a clear signal to seek out lower-angle or sheltered terrain.'}]
bottom_line: The wind is drifting snow into fresh slabs along ridges, saddles, and other prominent terrain features. Wind-sheltered terrain may hold isolated small slabs. You could trigger slides from gentler terrain below, above, and to the sides of steep slopes. Pull back from steep slopes if the snow surface cracks around your feet or machine.
discussion:
Put your hands at 10 and 2. We’ve been hot-rodding around for several weeks now, but as new snow starts to stack up on weak surfaces, it’s time to consider increasing your margin for safety. Snowfall totals in the last 24 hours were once again modest. Tunnel Ridge, in the northern Flathead Range, and Flattop, in Glacier National Park, picked up 2 inches of new snow. Other sites across the advisory area are reporting an inch or less. Upper-elevation winds have been sufficient to drift the fresh snow: Aeneas Ridge, in the Swan Range, has averaged over 30 mph with gusts exceeding 60 mph during the last 8 hours. 
A pair of observations from yesterday (Friday) found the new snow riding nicely on top of a supportable crust. Sarah and I were in the Swan Lake area. There, we found up to 6 inches of recent snow consisting of heavily rimed particles and graupel. Still, moderate gusts were drifting new snow into thin, stubborn wind slabs in favored locations along upper elevations ridgelines. Our most significant hazard, however, was still from thin coverage on our descent through middle elevations.
In locations favored by recent snowfall, you might find isolated slabs in wind-sheltered terrain. Incoming snowfall tonight will continue to build thicker slabs on top of weak layers and may increase avalanche danger into next week.

bottom_line:
Recent snowfall has improved the riding but increased the avalanche danger. Wind may be drifting snow into fresh thin slabs at upper elevations. Wind-sheltered terrain may hold isolated small slabs. You could trigger slides from gentler terrain below, above, and to the sides of steep slopes. 
The avalanche hazard is more widespread in the Swan Range, where more snow fell.
discussion:
Snowfall and precipitation totals for the system, which impacted our area Thursday into Friday, were disappointing. Noisy Basin, in the Swan Range, took home the lion's share with a respectable 1 inch of water; however, most other locations recorded just several inches of snow and around 1/3 inch of water. These totals should increase slightly before this system moves out of the area sometime mid-morning. Fortunately, unsettled weather continues through the weekend with widespread light snow and seasonably cool temperatures.
The new snow fell on weak faceted snow or surface hoar in many locations. If slabs develop on the underlying weak layer,  they might be more reactive to the weight of a rider than anticipated. There is the possibility of triggering slides from above, below, or to the sides of a steep slope. Slides may break wider and higher above you than expected. Today's triggered slabs may act more like a persistent slab than a storm or wind slab.
Today, we are listing storm slab, wind slab, and loose dry across the three zones, which are all new snow avalanche problems. As snow piles up through the weekend, the avalanche danger may increase as a thicker and stronger slab develops on top of weak snow. We expect to transition to a persistent slab problem in the next day or two.

author: Cam Johnson
bottom_line: Generally stable avalanche conditions exist today. If your travels take you to the highest peaks and ridgelines, look for and ride around small slabs of wind-drifted snow. Practice safe travel protocols by riding one at a time in steep terrain.
discussion:
I have enjoyed the long duration of low avalanche danger, but I am ready for change. Very little change will happen during daylight hours today, thus leading to yet another day of low danger. The welcomed change will begin tonight and will persist into the near future.
Today, expect more of the same avalanche conditions as the past week. Observations from the past week almost entirely consist of no signs of instability (no cracking and no collapsing). Experienced users did find small slabs of drifted snow on Great Northern in the Flathead Range yesterday (Wednesday). Other observations noted drifting snow, but slabs were hard to find or unreactive to the weight of a person. Although wind speeds will increase throughout the day today, there is little amounts of snow available to transport. If you travel into the alpine, ride around small dune-shaped drifts.
So, what changes are occurring tonight? The Swan Range may pick up 4 to 8 inches of snow and an additional 6 to 10 inches tomorrow. The Whitefish Range, Flathead Range, and southern Glacier National Park will likely see the lower end of those snow totals. This active weather pattern will continue through the upcoming week. The pattern shift will bring changing avalanche conditions and rising avalanche danger. Be sure to stay tuned since snow totals may vary widely throughout the forecast region.

author: Cam Johnson
bottom_line: Today will be a good day to get up high into the mountains. Recent observations continue to describe generally stable avalanche conditions. If your travels take you to upper rigdelines, steer around small slabs of drifted snow. Practice safe travel protocols by riding one at a time in steep terrain.
discussion:
Recent observations paint a similar portrait to the past week. Riders are finding no signs of instability, with 1 to 3 inches of recent snow above 6000 feet. Observers noted active wind transport at the highest ridgelines. With relatively benign weather today, I will expect to see similar reports.
Keep an eye on surface conditions and steer around small drifts at upper elevations. These will be more common in the alpine. Slightly warmer temperatures and a chance for sun may cause a few rollerballs or point releases on south-facing aspects. If this occurs, slides will be small due to the minimal amount of recent snow. Cloud cover will increase throughout the day, making this less likely to occur. 
A more active weather pattern will enter the forecast region on Thursday. Snowfall will start as flurries on Thursday, and precipitation rates will slowly increase over the weekend. An arctic air mass will enter the region on Saturday, making it difficult to predict how much snow will fall in the mountains. In some cases, arctics can enhance snowfall, and in other cases, it can turn the snow faucet off. Thankfully, this arctic air mass will only bring temperatures down into the teens. An active weather pattern will continue for the foreseeable future.
With this active weather pattern shift, I am beginning to think about shifting my mindset from “open season” to “stepping back.” This shift will happen when 8 or more inches of new snow stacks up. We’ve had nearly free reign in the hills for the greater part of three weeks. Changing avalanche conditions will be inevitable with a more active weather pattern. Stay tuned as information becomes more clear.

author: blase reardon
bottom_line: Travel conditions at upper elevations remain excellent for exploring high peaks, out-of-the-way drainages, and usually inaccessible terrain. Stay alert for isolated avalanche hazards like pockets of wind slab and sluffs that can carry you into a terrain trap. Shallow snow conditions make hitting hard things the most widespread hazard to you or your machine.
discussion:
You know it's an odd winter when our observations page and local chatter include almost no mention of the usual Middle Fork haunts and sidecountry go-tos around Whitefish Mountain Resort. Instead, we see regular obs from Comeau Pass and obscure drainages in the Whitefish Range and reports of repeated ascents of big ice lines and high peaks in Glacier Park. That's some creativity and energy. Take that, El Nino.
We've also had reports of two near-misses or avalanche accidents in the past three weeks, one of which resulted in minor injuries. While the backcountry is no horror-movie hell house, when you poke into the closets, you might find some trouble.  Gremlins, though, rather than Leatherface. That trouble consists primarily small slabs of drifted snow and long-running sluffs in steep, confined terrain. The shallow snowpack is weakening, but the evidence - or lack of evidence of instabilty - indicates buried weak layers are degrading and dormant. So if you head for high-consequences terrain - steep chutes, climbs that involve snowfields above or below rockbands, leeward ridges - stay alert for signs of danger like stiff surface snow that cracks around you, or sluffs that don't peter out promptly. 
And hey, it's a new year. The the waterfall portion of Russ' Street has been groomed, East Route is scheduled to open again to uphill traffic, and there's snow - not much - in the forecast for the weekend.

author: Sarah Williams
bottom_line: A welcome few inches of new snow has not increased the avalanche danger. Surface obstacles remain a hazard at all elevations. Enjoy your new year while managing your sluff, especially on slick crusts on sunny aspects.
discussion:
Happy New Year from all of us at the Flathead Avalanche Center. We’ve got some good news- snow and cool temperatures returned to the Flathead Valley on Sunday. Snow accumulations were limited to 1-2 inches in most of the forecast region. However, Stahl Peak and Noisy Basin SNOTELs each squeezed out 0.5 inches of Snow Water Equivalent (SWE). That likely amounts to 4 inches of settled snow in the areas near these stations.
You may trigger mostly harmless sluffs on very steep slopes today. Sunday's snow is resting on a slick crust on sunny aspects, which got baked over the past few days by above-average temperatures and sunshine.  Observers reported northerlies continue to hold dry, settled powder (Observation 1, Observation 2), which may be faceted. Sluffs won't involve enough snow to be consequential except in steep chutes, long-running gullies, above cliffs, or above terrain traps. 
Entering the New Year, our region’s Snow Water Equivalent - the water in the snow sitting on the ground - remains well below the median for this date at area SNOTELs. Locally, the northern Whitefish Range is doing best, with 83% at Stahl Peak. The Swan Range is recording 68% at Noisy Basin, with Flattop in central Glacier Park at 65%. As disappointing as this seems, we are actually doing much better than many other watersheds across the western US. Unfortunately, our SWE numbers would be much better if not for the warm start to the season. Looking at precipitation totals - the amount of water that's fallen from the sky - for the water year starting October 1, Stahl Peak is at 101% of the 30-year median.

author: Mark Dundas
bottom_line: Today's cooler and cloudier conditions are making for a generally stable snowpack. Surface obstacles remain a hazard at all elevations. You could trigger a small wet slide on a steep slope if you or your machine punches through the surface crust into the underlying moist snow.
discussion:
Saturday's temperatures were the warmest since the Atmospheric River impacted our area on December 5 and 6. Low elevations below the valley cloud deck remained cool, but temperatures above the stratus were uncomfortably above average. Upper elevation readings include 40F at the summit of WMR (6737 feet), 41F at Aeneas Ridge (7186 feet), and 43F at Snowslip (6950 feet). Badger Pass (6900 feet), just southeast of our advisory area, reached 50F with St Mary a balmy 58F. 
Temperatures overnight dipped below freezing at Aeneas Ridge following 31 hours above freezing. As of 0500, the above-freezing conditions continue for Stahl, Noisy, John F. Stevens Canyon, and Tunnel Ridge. 
This morning, we expect to find a thin surface crust capping perhaps 2-4 inches of wet snow on sunny aspects. Today's cooler and cloudier conditions diminish the rider-triggered wet loose slide hazard, and we have removed this from the problem list. However, a slight chance remains that you or your machine could punch through this crust and initiate a small wet slide on steep slopes. These should be harmless in size unless you are in a long-running gully or the debris carries you into a terrain trap.
We received two public observations on Saturday, a low total for a sunny holiday weekend. A party on the west face of the Great Northern accidentally triggered a wet loose slide on their descent. The slide appears relatively small, D 1.5, and harmless in its terrain. An experienced rider in the central Whitefish Range found dry settled powder on a northeast slope but intentionally triggered 5-10 cm deep wet loose slides on their southerly descent in the afternoon. They found the 12/7 crust stout at 7800 feet. I was in the Snyder Lake basin, basking in warm temperatures and listening to chunks of ice falling and trundling down the hillsides. No recent wet loose avalanche activity was observed.

author: Cam Johnson
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wet Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'unlikely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'Temperatures will climb above freezing to nearly 10,000 feet in elevation. Solar radiation and warm temperatures will wet the snow surface. Look for rollerballs out of steep, rocky terrain as early indicators for a loose wet avalanche problem. If you see signs of rollerballs or point releases, switch aspects or lower your slope angles.\xa0'}]
bottom_line: Maintain safe travel protocols by traveling one at a time through steep terrain. Select slopes with smooth runouts free of surface obstacles. If the snow surface becomes gloppy and wet, switch aspects to reduce the chance of triggering a small loose wet avalanche.
discussion:
A ridge of high pressure will lead to another beautiful day in the mountains and yet another day of dense fog in the valley. Temperatures are forecast to be the warmest of the past week, with freezing levels climbing up to nearly 10,000 feet. Cloud cover will slowly build throughout the day. Wind speed averages will remain in the single digits.
Over the past few days, warm temperatures and solar radiation have worked over the snow surface. Southerly-facing aspects have a melt-freeze crust on the surface, and snow on northerly-facing aspects has settled, and in some areas, a thin temperature crust exists. This should reduce the chances for a loose wet avalanche cycle. Although we expect loose wet activity to be unlikely and small in size, warm temperatures make us wary of removing it as a listed problem. Thankfully, it is an easy problem to identify and avoid. Rollerballs and pinwheels are precursors to loose wet avalanches. If you begin to see these occurring, switch aspects or reduce your slope angles. Warm temperatures may cause some loose wet activity on northerly-facing aspects.
During periods of extended low danger, it is easy to let your guard down and loosen safe travel protocols. Remember, triggering an avalanche is unlikely, not impossible. Snow coverage is currently thin, with plenty of surface obstacles that can cause trauma, even with a small slide. Experienced and professional users have found slabs of drifted snow or observed audible collapses, which altered their plans (observation, observation). These are good examples of keeping an eye on changing conditions and adapting to what you see.

bottom_line: Clearing skies and warm temperatures could cause small slides in wet snow. Seek out shaded slopes if you find moist snow or rollerballs. Thin slabs of wind-drifted snow can be found at upper elevations. Move to more sheltered or less steep terrain if the snow cracks around your feet or machine.
discussion:
With high pressure firmly in place, there’s not a whole lot of surprises. Here’s two in the back of my mind:

Today’s cloud cover burns off faster than anticipated causing the snow surface to become wet and sluff as I travel through mid-elevations.
I find evidence of wind-loading as I try to access an upper-elevation objective.

In either case, the resultant slide is likely to be small. They can, however, have out-sized consequences if you or your partner are carried through the abundant exposed rocks and vegetation, over a cliff, or into a terrain trap where even small amounts of snow can bury you. Reduce your risk by choosing planar slopes with clean runouts or by traveling one at a time where you’re most likely to get surprised.
Thanks to everyone who has continued to provide us with observations throughout these early-season doldrums. Even in quiet conditions, this information is extremely useful.  Weather, snow depths, surface conditions, maybe a photo..  It’s all good. Keep it coming!

bottom_line: Warming temperatures and forecasted sunshine may initiate small, wet loose slides in the surface snow. If you notice moist snow or rollerballs developing, move to a shaded aspect and drier conditions. In isolated locations, you may encounter thin slabs of wind-drifted snow. Cracking in the snow surface indicates you have found an unstable pocket of snow.
discussion:
Wednesday, a warm air mass on the east side of Glacier resulted in St Mary topping out at a balmy 54F. Warm upper-elevation air moved west over the Continental Divide with temperatures climbing above freezing at ridgetop locations in southern Glacier Park and the Flathead Range, with Tunnel Ridge wind (6796 feet) reaching 39F. In the Swan Range, upper elevations also warmed, with Aeneas Ridge (7186 feet) at freezing. The Whitefish Range escaped the warm air intrusion, with mid and upper-elevation stations remaining below freezing. Below the inversion, temperatures remained cool.
Warm conditions spread overnight to the previously cool mid-elevation band and the Whitefish Range. As of 0400, the Tunnel snow station  (5895 feet) is at 33F, 10-12 degrees warmer than yesterday afternoon, while Hornet (6500 feet) is at 31F. These temperatures are the warmest the surface snow has experienced since it fell five days ago.  The combination of this morning's temperatures and forecasted sunshine may initiate wet loose slides on sunny aspects. 
During the December 23 snowfall, the wind speeds were unusually light, with observations noting minimal evidence of wind transport over the past few days. Yesterday (Wednesday), Rob and blase visited Noisy and Jewel Basin in the Swan. They noted wind-transported snow, while the Aeneas Ridge weather station recorded speeds capable of transporting snow (>16 mph) since 11 a.m. Wednesday. An observer in the northern Whitefish Range found a reactive older wind slab on a northerly upper-elevation slope. The group was so concerned that they backed off a steep, larger objective. 
Generally, dry loose avalanches are not a concern 2-3 days after a snowfall. However, recent cold, clear nights have allowed the surface snow to facet, weaken, and not settle as fast as usual, as noted by the USGS crew in the Swan Range. A seasoned backcountry traveler triggered dry loose slides on steep northerly aspects in the Whitefish Range Wednesday. These sluffs are becoming more stubborn to trigger, and the resulting slides are smaller than a few days ago. The consequences of a small sluff can be magnified in a long-running gully or on a slope ending in a terrain trap.

author: guy zoellner
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Dry Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'unlikely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'northwest'], 'middle': [], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'On most slopes, the 4-6 inches of snow that fell before Christmas has had ample time to settle.\xa0 On very steep slopes, it’s still possible to trigger sluffs of loose dry snow. These will run on crusts that formed on 12/22.\xa0\xa0 If you feel the tug of steep terrain on a shaded aspect, double-check your runout and ability to manage sluffs.\xa0 While slow moving and not involving much snow, sluffs can be serious if they drag a person or machine into or over rocks or trees.'}, {'problem_type': ' Wet Loose', 'problem_number': 2, 'likelihood': 'unlikely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': [], 'middle': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'If skies are mostly clear, the surface snow on steep, sunny slopes may become wet and prone to sliding on the crust below it today.\xa0 Rollerballs, pinwheels and treebombs are clues that these conditions are developing. Wet snow sluffs will be relatively harmless unless they gouge below the crust or entrain more than 8 inches of snow. If temperatures warm more than forecast, this hazard could develop on isolated upper elevation slopes as well.'}]
bottom_line: Triggering loose snow avalanches will be today's primary avalanche concern. At upper elevations, these will mostly involve dry snow on very steep, shaded slopes. If skies are mostly clear, you can also trigger wet snow avalanches on sunny, mid-elevation slopes.  Avoid terrain where loose snow slides can carry you into a terrain trap.
discussion:
Return of the inversion!  Our weather pattern right now is a far cry from overnight dumps and faces full of powder. You will see lower valley stratus decks again today and balmy temperatures if you climb in elevation up to 6,000 feet.  Above there, expect near-freezing temperatures, dry snow and quality skiing and riding.
High pressure generally brings stable avalanche conditions along with it.  Today is a classic example of skiing/riding what is in front of you.  If you’re traveling into the upper elevations above 6,500 feet, you may be lured into shaded aspects to find the driest snow.  This snow fell several days ago and on most slopes, has had enough time to settle and bond with the old snow below.  Steep terrain, above 40 degrees, could still have some surface sluffing.
 
 If the snow that is in front of you starts to stick to your skins or fall out of the trees, you are most likely at mid elevations and starting to deal with a wet loose avalanche problem.  Temperatures at these elevations will be close to freezing today and just a few degrees of warm up could change the snow surface dramatically.  Pay attention to your location on slopes and move to more pleasant and stable terrain if you find yourself in this situation.
Winds have generally been calm and the availability of snow for transport is limited due to thin melt freeze crusts or settlement.  However, high peaks and ridgelines could still have isolated thin wind slabs.  Think about the consequence of the terrain below you before you drop onto a leeward aspect.  
And don’t forget to listen to the snowpack that you travel on.  Yesterday (Tuesday), I felt a collapse on a north aspect in the Whitefish Range at 7,300 feet.  Probing and digging confirmed a shallow snowpack and a thin 12/7 crust with a weak layer of facets/surface hoar below it.  Propagating results in our stability test (ECTP8 and ECTP23) showed that in very isolated terrain weakness may still lurk.
While we're in an extended period of mostly stable weather and avalanche conditions, you can still get into trouble. The steep terrain that's attractive in these conditions can dramatically magnify the consequences of even small avalanches. A good reminder of this problem is an avalanche accident that occurred on Mt. Brown on Saturday. Dec. 23. One member of a climbing team was overrun by debris from a large loose snow avalanche and injured. The party was aware of the seriousness of the terrain they entered and monitoring conditions. Even so, they were surprised by a sudden natural avalanche cycle. Low Danger doesn't equal no danger.

author: blase reardon
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Dry Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'unlikely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'west', 'northwest'], 'middle': [], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "You can still trigger sluffs of dry, loose snow on some shaded slopes at upper elevations. Most will be small and harmless. For them to be consequential, you'll need a slope approaching 40 degrees with more than about 8 inches of loose, dry snow. The most dangerous terrain will be confined chutes above terrain traps."}]
bottom_line: You can find great riding on wind-sheltered, shaded slopes above about 6000 feet. On isolated slopes, you might be able to trigger sluffs of loose, dry snow that can have serious consequences if they push you into a gully or over a cliff. You can minimize this hazard by picking unconfined lines with clean runouts.
discussion:
Monday's sun was kinda like finding a book under the tree instead of a bike. It's better than nothing. But a foot of new snow sure woulda been nice, Santa. We weren't that naughty. 
Monday, riders reported triggering sluffs of loose dry snow at upper elevations in the Swan Range. These were mostly harmless and became harder to trigger through the day, as the recent snow continued settling. Today, I expect the terrain where you can find this hazard is shrinking. Partly to mostly sunny skies the past two days have likely created a melt-freeze crust on sunny, mid- and upper-elevation terrain. This may be topped by very large surface hoar crystals. Steep, shaded slopes likely retain loose, dry snow at the surface. Sluffs that entrain snow can be dangerous, particularly if they push you into a consequence magnifier, like a cliff, a gully, trees, or dense brush. 
Ridgeline winds ticked up overnight and more gusts may arrive this afternoon. I doubt much snow is available to get moved around; the surface on windward slopes is the melt-freeze crust mentioned above. It'd be worth checking for thin, soft slabs of drifted snow at the tops of chutes, downwind of saddles, and in other terrain that collects wind-blown snow.
Wednesday looks to be sunny again, with temperatures warming to near or above freezing at mid elevations. Those conditions might be favorable for wet snow slides, except, again, for that melt-freeze crust on sunny terrain. If it's well-developed, it'll take a lot of energy to break down. Let us know if you're finding this when your out today. How thick is it? What elevations? Submit an obs online!
While it is Low danger, it remains worth keeping an eye out for isolated hazards and carrying avalanche rescue gear, as verse contributed by Dr. Dara Boilen reminds us:
So, you spent the day inside drinking egg nog T’was easy to do what with low valley fog All Santa brought us was more surface hoarIt’s like it don’t snow in Montana no more. You read in the obz it's good up high; so much fun! Tales of pow and something called the sun?You wanna head out, see if these rumors are true,Bask in the light under skies that are blue. It’s true there’s pow to ride if you get high enoughLook for wind effects and manage your sluffPick lines that aren't above nasty terrain trapsWatch for hazards in this shallow snow pack, braaaaap.Oh and remember as you shake off this extended fall haze Shovel, Probe, and beacon with the fresh triple A’s.

author: blase reardon
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Dry Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'middle': [], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "You're likely to trigger sluffs of dry, loose snow in steep terrain. Most will be small and harmless. However, on slopes with more than about 8 inches of loose, dry snow, triggered sluffs might entrain enough snow to be consequential. The most dangerous terrain will be confined chutes with continuously steep pitches above terrain traps."}]
bottom_line: In steep, upper-elevation terrain, be wary of sluffs of loose, dry snow. They can ruin your holiday week if they knock you off your feet or machine and dump you into a terrain trap. You can find great riding on slopes above about 6000 feet with runouts free of these consequences.
discussion:
'Twas the night before Christmas, when  all through the town
Few riders were riding; not much snow on the ground
New machines sat on trailers gathering dust
Favorite boards hung unused, edges gathering rust
The bros were stuck posting memes on the Instagram
"Yo; gotta chase my dreams; bouncing to Japan!"
And 'mama in her tights and me sitting on my ass
had just resigned ourselves to another zumba class
When midway down Moementum arose such a clatter
A dozen more riders also learning those little staubs matter.......
***************************
It's easy to see a grim picture out there. Saint Nick has definitely not delivered the storms and powder we put at the top of or wish-lists for the winter. A lotta people been naughty. 
He has, however, been jolly enough to toss a few stocking stuffers our way. A storm late last week was one. Up to 10 inches of snow accumulated across the region, accompanied by light to moderate winds. Favored areas included the northern Whitefish Range, the Jewel Basin area, and the Flathead Range. Observers in these spots have reported great riding conditions. Sunday's sun may have left south-facing slopes with a surface crust. The southern Whitefish and Apgar Ranges only got a few lumps of coal.
The past two days, sluffs of cohesionless snow have been the primary hazard. These are running in the recent snow, or on a friable melt-freeze crust below that. The recent snow is already faceting, exacerbating the problem. This danger is isolated to very steep slopes where even small slides can have outsized consequences.
Along with the snowfall, weather stations recorded light winds but some strong gusts. While we expected some wind slabs to form, few people have reported wind-affected snow, even in terrain that typically collects drifted snow. Sunday, I checked for - and didn't find - cohesive slabs of drifted snow before I committed to a steep chute. I'd continue that practice today if I were near a summit or ridge crest.
Another plus side to the otherwise discouraging weather pattern: early season weak layers have gone dormant. The isolated exceptions will be in terrain high enough that early December warming and rain didn't leave a stout melt-freeze crust a few feet below the snow surface. That's generally above 7500 or even 8000 feet. If you travel there, do some digging and look for active persistent weak layers. 
Uphill travel at Whitefish Mountain Resort is paused for Holiday Week and possibly longer

author: Sarah Williams
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wind Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "The storm has ended, but favored locations have plenty of snow for transport. Wind speeds have been unusually light, but we expect slabs of wind-drifted snow may be found on the downwind side of a ridgeline or saddle and the sidewalls of cross-loaded gullies. Their size and thickness will increase as you gain elevation. Evidence that winds have been at work includes scoured or scalloped surfaces, dunes, and snow denser than what you previously moved through. Cracking beneath your feet signifies you have found an unstable slab.\xa0 In areas favored by Saturday's snowfall, you may encounter slabs of snow on slopes sheltered from the wind. Cracking in the surface indicates you should retreat to a lower-angle slope. \xa0"}]
bottom_line:
Westerly winds and new snow are building wind slabs above 6000 feet. As you travel, look for dune-shaped pillows, cracking under your skis and machine, or feel changes in the density of the snow. Give yourself an early Christmas present and choose to ski and ride in more sheltered locations, avoiding wind-stiffened snow.
Locations that received less recent snow will have a lower danger. These include the mountains in the southern Whitefish Range, the Apgar Range, and possibly central Glacier Park.
discussion:
Yesterday’s early Christmas storm overperformed in parts of the Flathead Range, the northern Whitefish Range, and the Swan Range. Tunnel Ridge weather station recorded 10 inches of snow, Stahl Peak gained 0.7 SWE (snow water equivalent), and Noisy Basin picked up 1 inch of SWE. The least amount of snow was in the southern Whitefish Range, where WMR reported 2 inches.
An observer in the northern Whitefish confirmed snowfall ranged from 4 to 8 inches and noted dry loose slides that ran far on the underlying crust. They also observed one small soft slab avalanche. I was in the Apgar Range yesterday, where I found 3 inches of moist new snow at 6400 feet. 
We are continuing to list wind slab as today’s primary hazard but, due to the overproducing storm, have bumped the danger level up across the board. Yesterday (Saturday), thin storm slabs may have formed in areas favored by snowfall. However, most of the precipitation ended early or mid-day yesterday, giving these features a bit of time to heal. Dry loose slides may be the most common avalanche you encounter today. These will have the ability to travel fast and far on the underlying crust. Manage your sluff, especially in long-running gullies. Limit your time spent above terrain traps if you encounter this problem.
From our partner Whitefish Mountain Resort: Due to unfavorable snow conditions and increased pressures from holiday visitation, Whitefish Mountain Resort and the U.S. Forest Service are implementing a pause on all uphill skiing traffic within the resort's boundaries, effective Christmas Day, Monday, Dec. 25.

author: Mark Dundas
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wind Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'Several inches of fresh snow and gusty winds may form small, thin slabs reactive to the weight of a rider or their machine. These features may be found just below the downwind side of a ridgeline or saddle and on the sidewalls of cross-loaded gullies. Their size and thickness will increase as you gain elevation. Active wind transport, dunes, a scalloped surface, and dense fresh snow indicate that wind has been or is at work. Cracking beneath your feet signifies you have found an unstable slab. Utilize terrain sheltered from the wind as your safer alternative.'}]
bottom_line: Light snow and winds may form thin slabs reactive to your weight. As you gain elevation, these will increase in size and thickness and will be largest in areas favored by snowfall. Cracking beneath you or your machine indicates you have found an unstable slab. Terrain sheltered from the wind offers a safer alternative.
discussion:
A welcome cold front arrived late last night, dropping temperatures and depositing light snow. Overnight snow totals at ridgetop locations are modest, with 1-3 inches reported as of 0500 this morning. Stahl Peak in the northern Whitefish Range seems to be an outlier, with 0.7 inches of SWE recorded overnight. In many locations, snow fell on a slick surface crust, which may inhibit bonding. Wind speeds are strong enough for transport and variable from the SE-SW-NW. Above 6000 feet, small, thin slabs of wind-drifted snow may be encountered in areas favored by snowfall and wind. The size of today's slabs will probably be too small to bury you; however, even taking a ride in a small avalanche could have a nasty outcome due to all of the exposed surface obstacles.
Before the arrival of the cooler air, temperatures were downright balmy at all elevations for the workweek. The mid-elevation Noisy Basin SNOTEL (6040 feet) remained above freezing from early Monday morning until Friday evening, or 110 hours. Marias Pass (5230 feet) recorded 39 F or higher throughout the winter solstice or over 40 hours. These unusually warm temperatures melted our thin snowpack, especially at low and mid-elevations. In John F. Stevens Canyon, the valley floor Shed 4 station (4557 feet) is snow-free, and Marias Pass reports a mere 3 inches. In the Swan Range, a dedicated observer noted snow on the ground nearly disappeared below 5000 feet on northerly aspects and below 6000 feet on the west and southwest aspects.  
On Wednesday, warm air and rain initiated a small, wet loose avalanche cycle on all aspects at mid-elevations (Observation). The arrival of cooler air has likely left a crust this morning on mid and upper-elevation slopes that were previously wet and now have frozen rollerballs and debris.

author: Rob Millspaugh
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wet Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'You can trigger sluffs of dense, wet snow on slopes steeper than about 38 degrees. These can be dangerous on slopes above terrain traps and will run farther slopes without low-angle benches that can slow moving debris. Rollerballs that fan out from your machine or boards are a clear sign that the surface snow is wet. Step back to lower-angled terrain if you see these conditions. This hazard may be more widespread on shaded slopes where the surface snow is getting wet for the first time.\xa0'}]
bottom_line: Continued warm temperatures mean you can trigger wet snow avalanches on very steep slopes below roughly 7000 feet. These could injure or burry you if they carry you into a terrain trap, like a gully, dense brush, trees or rocks. Move off of - and out from under - steep slopes if you trigger, or notice, recent roller balls and pinwheels.
discussion:
We’re on a hot streak. Or a warm streak at least. The temperature at Noisy Basin in the Swan Range hasn’t dropped below freezing since Monday, December 18th at 0700. Temperatures will remain mild until late today, making it a full work week of warm. Most mid-elevation weather stations have faired similarly. Meanwhile, temperatures at upper elevations have hovered just below freezing this week.
With extended mild temperatures at middle elevations, it’s likely that many steep slopes in this elevation band have already been worked over by rollerball or loose wet activity. Shaded aspects and upper elevations, however, still harbor dry snow that is transitioning to wet and will be ripe for sluffing. If you climb above 7000 feet in your search for dry snow, recall that these highest reaches of the advisory area are where buried weak layers are most likely to have survived previous warm-ups. Dig down and verify that there is a stout 12/7 crust capping early-season snow. In its absence, dig a little deeper to identify any buried weak layers, like the 12/1 surface hoar. This weak layer is identifiable by a grey stripe in the wall of a snowpit. If you find this poor snowpack structure (i.e. thin breakable crust and a layer of buried surface hoar), move to a different slope or aspect. Thankfully, this poor structure is proving hard to find.
Fortunately, warm temperatures will clock out for the weekend as a cold front arrives this evening. This system will deliver 2 to 5 inches of snow and enough wind to blow it around. If you’re the nocturnal type you might encounter some new snow problems, otherwise the incoming precipitation shouldn’t be a factor during today’s travel.

author: blase reardon
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wet Loose', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': [], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'You can trigger sluffs of dense, wet snow on mid-elevation slopes steeper than about 38 degrees. These can be dangerous on slopes above terrain traps or slopes without low-angle benches that can slow moving debris. Rollerballs that fan out from your machine or boards are a reliable sign that the surface snow is wet. Step back to lower-angled terrain if you see these conditions. This hazard may be more widespread on shaded slopes where the surface snow is getting wet for the first time.\xa0'}]
bottom_line: It's the winter solstice, so of course the primary avalanche concern is... triggering wet snow avalanches on very steep slopes at mid-elevations. These may ruin your holidays if they carry you into a terrain trap, like a gully, dense brush, trees or rocks. Recent or triggered roller balls on steep slopes are a reliable indicator that these slides are possible.
discussion:
They said there'll be snow at ChristmasThey said there'll be peace on EarthBut instead it just kept on raining
                                - "I Believe in father Christmas," Emerson, Lake & Palmer
Another 48 hours of above-freezing temperatures and light rain across the region is testing my faith in Father Christmas. And Cold Miser, Jack Frost, Boreas, Skadi, and any other deity associated with snow. On the plus side, however, the warm snowpack is steadily degrading any buried weak layers. If - once - winter arrives, we'll be dealing with new snow avalanche concerns. And despite the soggy conditions, we found decent turns Wednesday east of WMR.
For the solstice - yes, the winter solstice, which is tonight at 7:27 - wet snow avalanches are the primary avalanche concern. This hazard is specific to very steep, mid-elevation slopes with more than a few inches of wet snow at the surface. It's isolated at upper elevations, where temperatures have mostly remained below freezing. And also isolated at low elevations, because, well, the snow cover is too thin to pose much danger on most slopes. 
The Persistent Slab avalanche hazard that dominated concerns for the first few weeks of the month remains a lingering concern above about 7000 feet. A layer of surface hoar and facets is buried 1 to 2 feet deep (12/1) and *might* be sensitive to a person's weight on the isolated slopes where it's not shielded by a sandwich of ice layers and refrozen wet snow. The forecast team has looked for that structure in the past few days but did not find many signs of instability in the northern Whitefish Range, east of Whitefish Mountain Resort, the northern Swan Range, or Tunnel Ridge in the Flathead Range. If I made it above the rain-snow line today and tomorrow, I'd still check for the 12/1 surface hoar and facets before committing to steep, consequential terrain. Particularly on northerly slopes, where the layer was more developed and the 12/7 crust may be thinner.
Maybe a little reverse psychology is in order. No more snow dances or ski sacrifices in bonfires. Maybe dancing to reggaeton, Bad Bunny, or Jimmy Buffet in hula skirts and flip flops.

author: guy zoellner
bottom_line: Exercise normal caution today. The biggest hazard to you or your machine may be hitting stumps, rocks or trees.  Minimize your risk by traveling with a partner, exposing one person at a time in steep terrain, and doing so while carrying your rescue gear.
discussion:
If you like mashed potatoes with Christmas dinner, yesterday was your day. Generally, weather stations across the board reported temperatures above freezing in the last 24 hours. The exception are weather stations above roughly 6,700 feet in elevation. Expect warm temperatures to continue today with a slight possibility of mixed rain and snow.
The lack of snow below 6500 feet makes travel into the alpine tough, especially as snow for machines or skis to slide on continues to disappear with warm temperatures. Despite the battles on lower elevation slopes, people have found relatively safe and enjoyable snow conditions above 6,500 ft.
Yesterday (Tuesday), I was in the Swan range and found small wind slabs at upper elevation ridgelines. These were thin (4-6 inches), isolated directly below the ridge, and did not propagate very wide. This is a great example of being aware and open to changing conditions. Read the terrain and plan your travel accordingly. While small and isolated, if a person were to kick one of these off in steep, consequential terrain, the outcome could be season-ending.
Increased temperatures yesterday and overnight may have caused wet loose avalanches in the middle elevation band (5000-6500 feet). Plenty of rollerballs will be on display if you venture out today. Low snow in this elevation band makes these mostly benign. However, being caught on a steep slope with plenty of surface roughness (i.e. stumps, rocks, and logs) could injure you, or damage your machine. 
You will also notice that we’ve dropped Persistent Slab as a problem. Poor structure and low danger can sometimes go together. This is a case in point. While the buried surface hoar beneath the 12/7 crust is showing up in snow pits, there haven’t been any signs of instability or reported avalanches associated with it in the last two weeks. There exists the possibility that a person may find this structure visiting slopes that face NW-N-NE above 7,000 feet. If the crust is thin and you’re getting unstable results in tests, that is a good indicator you may have encountered one of these areas. Before committing to a slope like this, consider the consequence of a 1 to 2 foot slab avalanche in the terrain you’ve chosen.

bottom_line: Signs of unstable snow have dropped off over the past week. Despite the good stability, on northerly facing slopes at upper elevations, a weak snowpack structure still exists. If you decide to enter steep terrain, the best practice is to do so one at a time while having a spotter in a safe location.
discussion:
Observations over the past week have generally described stable avalanche conditions. The only reported red flags are signs of localized cracking around skis or boards, which predominately occurred in wind-drifted snow. The recent avalanche activity has been relatively benign. We received reports of mostly small loose snow avalanches, and one skier-triggered wind slab avalanche last week. This slab avalanche was also small in size. If you have not yet, take a look at the Visual tab on the new observation platform, located here. This is an incredibly useful tool that helps paint a picture of what everyone is reporting to us out in the mountains. 
Although we are seeing generally safe avalanche conditions, we are also still finding a weak snowpack structure in areas. The type of structure we are concerned about is where the 12/7 crust becomes thin and breakable, and where a weak layer of surface hoar lurks below. You can find this weak layer by first locating the crust. The crust is a good identifier in the snowpack, and is commonly found buried beneath 1 to 2 feet of soft snow. If you dig below this crust, roughly 1 to 8 inches further down, you can sometimes find this finicky weak layer by its visual appearance. Surface hoar is typically seen by a grey stripe in the snowpack. This layer of surface hoar has ranged from very easy to find, to more difficult to find. This is the setup I am still steering away from. 
The areas where a person may find this problem are in the uppermost reaches of the forecast area, and in areas where we have a deeper snowpack. Some of those areas that come to mind are the crest of the Flathead Range, around Mt. Aeneas in the Swan Range, and the higher peaks in the N. Whitefish Range. Most other areas have a thinner snowpack, or the 12/7 crust is thick and supportable, thus making it less likely for this problem to exist.
